topics.name: Define Forensic Medicine. (B.A-15)
topics.answer: Forensic medicine is the science that relates to the application of medical knowledge for the dispensation of justice in a court of law. It deals with applying the knowledge of every branch of medicine whenever necessary for reaching a decision in legal affairs, concerning both civil and criminal cases, to aid in the administration of justice.

topics.name: What are the aims and objectives of Forensic Medicine? (B.A-27)
topics.answer: The aims of forensic medicine include the application of medical and paramedical knowledge to aid in the administration of justice. Furthermore, it is used by legal authorities for the solution of legal problems, such as applying medical knowledge in deciding cases related to injuries, murder, suicide, accidents, sexual offenses, and poisoning.

topics.name: What are the goals of Forensic Medicine? (B.A-50)
topics.answer:
The goals of forensic medicine are centered on supporting the legal process and criminal investigation:

• Application of medical knowledge in legal matters concerning both civil and criminal cases.
• Aiding in the investigation of cases that are sudden, unexpected, suspicious, or unnatural.
• Examining various victims (living or dead) to provide a medicolegal opinion regarding the cause and nature of injuries and death.
• Identifying individuals through various medical examinations, thereby assisting in crime investigation.

topics.name: Define Clinical Forensic Medicine and give example. (B.A-6, 23)
topics.answer:
Clinical forensic medicine involves applying clinical medical knowledge for law enforcement and the judiciary when a medical professional encounters them.

Examples of the scope of clinical forensic medicine include the examination of adult sexual assault victims and the documentation and interpretation of medical evidence from victims of crime. It also includes examining patients for injuries.

topics.name: Define Forensic Medicine & Medical Jurisprudence. (B.A-11)
topics.answer:
• Forensic Medicine: This is the science concerned with the application of medical knowledge to aid the administration of justice.

• Medical Jurisprudence: This is the subject concerned with the knowledge of law in relation to the practice of medicine. It deals specifically with the legal aspects of practicing medicine, especially the responsibilities arising from the physician-patient relationship, such as consent, medical ethics, and serious professional misconduct.

topics.name: Tell the difference between Forensic Medicine and Medical Jurisprudence. (B.A-13)
topics.answer:
| Traits | Forensic Medicine / Legal Medicine | Medical Jurisprudence |
|---|---|---|
| Deals with | Application of medical knowledge to aid the administration of justice. | Application of knowledge of law in the practice of medicine. |
| Use | Used by the court for the solution of legal problems. | Used by members of the medical profession for their guidance. |
| Application | Can be applied to all people. | Can only be applied to members of medical professions. |
| Relation to medical practitioner | Brings medical men into contact with courts of law. | Deals with the relationship between doctor and patient, and doctor and society. |
| Sub-divisions | It has some subdivisions. | It has no subdivisions. |

topics.name: Why doctors of all categories need fair knowledge in Forensic Medicine? (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
Doctors need a fair knowledge of forensic medicine for several reasons:
• To accurately express their opinions in a court of law.
• For professional self-safeguard, enabling the doctor to know what actions are lawful and what are unlawful.
• To understand and follow their own code of conduct regarding what is mandatory and what is prohibited.

topics.name: What do you mean by Forensic Pathology? (B.A-9, 21)
topics.answer: Forensic pathology is a branch of Forensic medicine that deals with medical and pathological principles for determining the manner and causes of death. It assists in determining the cause and manner of death in circumstances that are sudden, unnatural, suspicious, unexplained, unexpected, and medically unattended.

topics.name: Define Forensic Thanatology. (B.A-24)
topics.answer: Forensic thanatology is the science concerned with the scientific study of death, including the types of death, the various events or changes that occur in the cadaver, and their medicolegal significance.

topics.name: Define Forensic Ballistics. (B.A-20)
topics.answer: Forensic ballistics is the science dealing with the investigations of firearms, ammunition, and the problems arising from their use.

topics.name: Define Consent? Tell the types of it. (B.A-1)
topics.answer:
Consent is a free & voluntary agreement, approval or permission for compliance of some act. The fundamental importance of consent lies in the fact that to examine, treat, or operate upon a patient without consent is considered assault in law, even if the action is medically beneficial and done in good faith.

The types of consent are primarily divided into:
1. Implied consent: This type of consent is not expressed by words or in writing but is implied by the patient's actions. For example, when a patient visits a doctor's chamber and narrates their complaints, it is implied that they have given consent for a physical examination.
2. Expressed consent: This type is specifically stated by the patient. Expressed consent can be either Verbal or Written.

topics.name: What is Dichotomy? (B.A-1)
topics.answer: Dichotomy, also known as fee splitting, means receiving or giving commission or other benefits to a professional colleague, manufacturer, or trader in drugs, appliances, a chemist, or a dentist. This practice is classified as serious professional infamous conduct. A physician is prohibited from giving, soliciting, or receiving any gift, gratuity, commission, or bonus in consideration for referring, recommending, or procuring any patient for treatment.

topics.name: What do you mean by Fee sharing? Tell its importance. (B.A-2, 6)
topics.answer:
Fee sharing refers to the sharing of fees generally received by a combined operation among the surgeon, their assistants, nurses, and bearers, especially when working in private clinics.

The medico-legal importance of fee sharing is that it is considered ethical, and therefore, there is no question of erasure of the practitioner's name from the registration due to this practice.

topics.name: Define medical ethics? Tell any 4 codes of medical ethics. (B.A-3, 10)
topics.answer:
Medical ethics deals with the moral principles which should guide the members of the medical profession in the course of their practice of medicine and their dealings with their patients and other members of the profession. Alternatively, it is concerned with the moral principles that should guide members of the medical profession in their dealings with each other, their patients, and the State.

Four key codes from the International Code of Medical Ethics/Geneva Declaration include:
1. I solemnly pledge myself to consecrate my life to the service of humanity.
2. I will give to my teachers the respect and gratitude that is their due.
3. The health of my patient will be my first consideration.
4. I will respect the secrets that are confided in me, even after the patient has died.

topics.name: What is professional infamous conduct? What are the acts that constitute it? (B.A-3, 18, 20)
topics.answer:
Professional infamous conduct (PIC) is defined as "that act of a medical man, done in the pursuit of his profession, with regard to which it would be reasonably regarded as disgraceful and dishonorable by his professional brethren of good repute and competence". This conduct implies an abuse of the professional position.

Acts that constitute professional infamous conduct include:
• Adultery.
• Criminal abortion.
• Dichotomy/fee splitting.
• Covering (assisting an unqualified person to treat or operate).
• Advertising.
• Using touts or agents for procuring patients.
• Issuing a false certificate.

topics.name: A color blind driver came to your chamber. Will you disclose the matter to his master? (B.A-3)
topics.answer: Yes, the matter should be disclosed to the appropriate authority if the driver refuses to accept the advice to stop driving. This disclosure is permitted under the concept of privileged communication. A doctor has the right to inform proper authority of a condition like color blindness in individuals such as a driver or pilot. Disclosure is justified in the interest of the community or the state.

topics.name: Tell the punishment given by Bangladesh Judicial System. (B.A-4)
topics.answer:
The punishments/sentences authorized by law in criminal cases include:
• Death sentence.
• Imprisonment for life.
• Imprisonment, which can be rigorous (with hard labour including solitary confinement) or simple.
• Forfeiture of property.
• Fine.

topics.name: Define medical negligence. Tell its types. (B.A-4, 11)
topics.answer:
Medical negligence (or Professional negligence/Malpraxis) is defined as absence of reasonable care & skill, or willful negligence of a medical professional in the treatment of a patient which causes bodily injury or death of the patient.

The sources define several types of negligence, including:
• Civil malpraxis / negligence (e.g., failure to obtain informed consent, making a wrong diagnosis).
• Criminal malpraxis / negligence (e.g., performing criminal abortion, amputation of the wrong limb).
• Contributory negligence (concurrent negligence by the patient and doctor).
• Corporate negligence (failure of the institution to follow established standards).

topics.name: How can you differentiate between Medical certificate & Medicolegal report? (B.A-4)
topics.answer:
Both are considered forms of medical documentary evidence.

• Medical Certificate: This is a general document related to health or administrative facts, such as birth, death, age, or health certificates.
• Medicolegal Report: This is a report prepared by a doctor usually on the requisition from some authorized person (like a Judge, Magistrate, or police officer). They are typically related to civil or criminal cases and include documents such as a post-mortem report, injury report, or report on rape.

topics.name: Define evidence. What are the medical documentary evidences? (B.A-5, 25)
topics.answer:
Evidence means any statement in relation to a matter or fact under inquiry or a document or something, for the inspection of the court. It includes all statements made by witnesses and documents produced for court inspection.

The medical documentary evidences are:
• Medical certificates (e.g., Birth certificates, Death certificates, Age certificates).
• Medicolegal reports (e.g., Post-mortem report, Injury report, Report on rape).
• Dying declaration (oral or written statement of a dying person).
• Dying deposition (statement made under oath in the presence of the magistrate and accused).

topics.name: Dr. 'X' is using agent for procuring patients. What should be the punishment? (B.A-5, 10)
topics.answer:
Using touts or agents for procuring patients (or association with unqualified people) constitutes professional infamous conduct (PIC).

Punishment for professional infamous conduct is given by the BMDC and may include a Warning, Temporary suspension, or Penal erasure (also called professional death sentence). Penal erasure means the permanent removal of the doctor's name from registration.

topics.name: What is Privileged communication? Give examples. (B.A-6, 16, 23)
topics.answer:
Privileged communication is a statement made bonafide upon any subject matter by a doctor to the concerned authority, due to their duty to protect the interests of the community or the State. The communication must only be made to persons directly concerned.

Examples of conditions under which professional secrecy can be disclosed (privileged communication):
1. To notify authorities of cases of certain infectious diseases (like cholera or chicken pox).
2. To inform proper authority if an individual (such as a driver or pilot) is suffering from color blindness.
3. Disclosure in a court of law for the interest of justice.
4. To report information about the commission of a crime (e.g., treating a bomb or firearm injury case) to law enforcement.
5. To advise the cook to abstain from cooking if suffering from an infectious disease, and informing the employer in the interest of the community.

topics.name: Define Court. What are the types of Courts in Bangladesh? (B.A-7, 12)
topics.answer:
A Court includes all judges and magistrates and all persons except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.

The types of Courts in Bangladesh include:
• Supreme Court (which has the Appellate division and High court division).
• Criminal courts (e.g., Courts of session, Courts of magistrates).
• Civil courts (e.g., Court of district judge, Family court, Labour court).

topics.name: What is professional secrecy? Tell example. (B.A-7, 23)
topics.answer:
Professional secrecy (or Medical confidentiality) is an implied contract between the medical practitioners and his patients whereby a doctor is obliged to keep as inviolable secrets all that comes to his knowledge about patients in course of his professional work.

Examples of professional secrecy:
• A doctor should not discuss the illness of his patient with others without the consent of the patient.
• In a divorce case on medical ground, a doctor should not furnish any information without having consent of the patient concerned.
• If a domestic servant is examined at the master's request, the doctor should not disclose any facts about the illness to the master without the consent of the servant, even though the master is paying the fee.
• A doctor should maintain secrecy about certain facts disclosed during a post mortem examination whose disclosure might affect the reputation of the deceased or relatives.

topics.name: What is covering? Tell punishment of this condition. (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
Covering means assisting someone who has no medical qualification to attend, treat or perform an operation on some person in case requiring professional discretion or skill.

Covering constitutes professional infamous conduct (PIC). Punishment given by BMDC for PIC includes a Warning, Temporary suspension, or Penal erasure (professional death sentence).

topics.name: Define medical etiquette and give example. (B.A-12, 19)
topics.answer:
Medical etiquette is the subject concerned with the conventional laws & customs of courtesy which are generally followed between members of the medical profession. It deals with the conventional laws of courtesy observed among members of the medical profession.

An example is that a doctor should not criticize or denigrate the professional ability of another doctor while dealing with a patient.

topics.name: In between challan & inquest report which one is more valuable? (B.A-12)
topics.answer: The Inquest Report is arguably the more valuable document regarding the facts of the case. The Inquest Report documents the investigation into the cause of a suspicious death, containing a brief history about the dead body and the signature of the witness. Furthermore, a Magistrate's inquest (which produces a report) is considered superior to a police inquest. The Chalan is merely the written requisition submitted to the authorized medical officer requesting an autopsy.

topics.name: Define malpraxis. Tell its components. (B.A-13, 22)
topics.answer:
Malpraxis (or Professional negligence) is defined as the absence of reasonable care & skill, or willful negligence of a medical professional in the treatment of a patient which causes bodily injury or death of the patient.

The criteria that must be satisfied to establish liability for negligence (the components) are:
1. Duty: The existence of a duty of care by the doctor.
2. Dereliction: Failure on the part of the doctor to maintain applicable standard of care and skill.
3. Direct causation: The failure of duty must lead to damage.
4. Damage: The damage should be of a type that would have been foreseen by a reasonable physician.

topics.name: What do you mean by BMDC and BMA? (B.A-14, 21)
topics.answer:
• BMDC (Bangladesh Medical and Dental Council): This is a Government body. Its functions include the Recognition of Medical and Dental Qualifications, Registration of Medical and Dental Practitioners, and disciplinary functions like the power to remove names from the register.
• BMA (Bangladesh Medical Association): This is a non-government organization. It is an organization that works to establish the rights and privileges of registered medical practitioners. All its members are elected.

topics.name: Tell the difference between dying deposition and dying declaration. (B.A-14, 22)
topics.answer:
| Trait | Dying Declaration | Dying Deposition (Bed side court) |
|---|---|---|
| Recorded by | Magistrate or a doctor. | Always by a Magistrate. |
| Oath | Is not necessary. | Is necessary (statement made under oath). |
| Accused presence | Not necessary. | Accused or his lawyer must be present. |
| Cross-examination | No opportunity for cross-examination. | Cross-examination is possible. |
| Legal value if patient survives | No value. | Has value. |

topics.name: Define conduct money. When it is provided? (B.A-16)
topics.answer:
Conduct money is the fee offered or paid to a witness in civil cases, at the time of serving the summons to meet the expenses for attending the court.

It is provided in civil cases to the witness by the party that has called him. In criminal cases, although no fee is paid when the summons is served, the doctor is entitled to claim conveyance charges and a daily allowance after attending the court.

topics.name: Taking informed written consent is mandatory - Tell some conditions. (B.A-17)
topics.answer:
Informed written consent is mandatory prior to:
• Administration of general anesthetic.
• Any procedure beyond routine physical examination, such as an operation, blood transfusion, or collection of blood.
• Examining a victim in a criminal case.
• Examination of a rape victim.
• Examination in medicolegal cases of pregnancy, delivery and abortion.
• Performing a major operation (failure to take written consent constitutes PIC).

topics.name: Define Professional death sentence. (B.A-24)
topics.answer: Professional death sentence, also referred to as penal erasure, is defined as the permanent erasure or removal of the name of a registered medical practitioner from registration as a disciplinary action. It is imposed as a punishment for serious professional infamous conduct (PIC).

topics.name: What do you mean by vicarious responsibilities? (B.A-24)
topics.answer: Vicarious liability (or vicarious responsibilities) means liability of a person due to the act of another. In medical practice, this signifies the responsibility of a superior medical or paramedical employee for the negligent act of his subordinate employees or those under training who are expected to work under the superior's guidance.

topics.name: What is contributory negligence? (B.A-25)
topics.answer: Contributory negligence is defined as concurrent negligence by the patient and doctor resulting in delayed recovery or harm to the patient. It is characterized as any unreasonable conduct or absence of ordinary care on the part of the patient or their attendant which combined with the doctor's negligence, contributed to the injury. Examples include the patient failing to give an accurate medical history or refusing to follow suggested treatment.

topics.name: Tell genital signs of virginity. (B.A-2)
topics.answer:
The genital signs of virginity include:
• Labia Majora: They are typically adjacent to each other, fully developed, firm, elastic, and completely close the vaginal orifice. Even upon abduction of the legs, the vaginal orifice remains completely closed.
• Labia Minora: These are usually covered by the labia majora and are soft, small, pinkish, and elastic.
• Hymen: It is generally intact, rigid, and inelastic, with distinct and regular edges. The opening is narrow, hardly allowing the tip of the little finger to pass.
• Fourchette and Posterior Commissure: Both the fourchette (where the lower portions of the labia minora fuse) and the posterior commissure are intact.
• Vagina: It is typically narrow and tight, possessing walls that are approximated and rugose.

topics.name: How can you identify a driver from RTA? (B.A-3, 25)
topics.answer:
Identifying a driver, especially if the body is mutilated or part of a mass disaster like a Road Traffic Accident (RTA), relies on key identification data:
• Physical Findings: Identifying and documenting all injuries, particularly those consistent with occupant position (e.g., impact marks from the steering wheel or dashboard), are crucial.
• Acquired Peculiarities: Occupational marks, tattoo marks, and scar marks found on the individual serve as data for identification.
• Personal Data: Essential traits include age, sex, stature, and hair features.
• Forensic Techniques: The identification process relies on the surest methods such as Fingerprint and DNA print.

topics.name: State medicolegal importance of 18 years of age. (B.A-4, B.B-2, 8)
topics.answer:
The medicolegal importance of attaining 18 years of age includes several civil and criminal responsibilities:
• Attainment of Majority: A person reaches majority at this age, except if they are under the guardianship of a court.
• Marriage (Female): It is the minimum permissible age for a woman to enter into a marriage contract.
• Legal Consent: A person above 18 years of age can give valid consent to suffer any harm that is neither intended nor known to cause death or grievous hurt.
• Will and Franchise: A mentally sound person above 18 years can make a valid will and exercise the right to cast vote.
• Kidnapping/Prostitution (Female): Taking a girl below 18 years from the custody of her legal guardian is considered kidnapping, and procuring a girl under this age for prostitution is an offense.
• Employment: A person above 18 years can be employed in any authorized job in a factory.

topics.name: What are the findings in a girl habituated to sexual intercourse? (B.A-6)
topics.answer:
A girl habituated to sexual intercourse (showing signs of defloration) may exhibit the following findings:
• Genital Area: The labia majora are often not apposed to each other, and the vaginal orifice may be visible. The labia minora are exposed and separated.
• Vagina: The vagina is wider, and the vaginal walls are relaxed. The natural folds, or rugae, are less folded. The fossa navicularis disappears.
• Hymen: The hymen may be torn or, if intact, it is usually loose, elastic, and soft, possessing a wide opening sufficient to admit two or more fingers. The remnants of a ruptured hymen may form Carunculae hymenalis.
• Clitoris: The clitoris may be large.
• Extra-genital Signs: The breasts are typically large and pendulous; the nipples are large, and the areolas are larger and darker in color. Signs of previous pregnancy or delivery may also be present.

topics.name: How will you detect the criminal who removed the tattoo mark? (B.A-7)
topics.answer: If a person, particularly a criminal, attempts to conceal their identity by artificially obliterating or removing a tattoo mark, the pigments can often still be detected in the nearest lymph glands. The physician can also attempt to erase the mark using methods like surgery, electrolysis, corrosive substances, or laser beams.

topics.name: How can you differ muslim male from hindu male? (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
The difference between a Muslim male and a Hindu male can be based on certain traditional practices:
• Circumcision: A Muslim male is circumcised. A Hindu male is typically not circumcised.
• Hindu Cultural Marks: A Hindu male may have a sacred thread, a neck lace of wooden beads, a cast mark on the forehead, a tuft of hair on the back of the head, or piercing of ear lobules.

topics.name: What is easiest and surest methods of identification? (B.A-9)
topics.answer:
The following are noted as the easiest and surest methods of identification:
• Easiest method of identification: Photograph.
• Surest method of identification: Fingerprint and DNA print.

topics.name: How will you determine the stature of a person from a dissected arm? (B.A-10)
topics.answer: To determine the stature (height) of a person from a dissected arm, forensic experts use the principles of forensic osteology. The arm contains the humerus, which is a long bone. Long bones are examined to determine the stature of an individual. By measuring the length of the long bone (such as the humerus) and applying relevant formulas, the height of the victim can be approximated.

topics.name: Define Forensic Dactylography. Types of fingerprints? (B.A-18, B.B-5)
topics.answer:
• Forensic Dactylography: This is defined as the study of fingerprint as a method of identification. It is the process of taking the impression of papillary ridges of the fingertips for the purpose of identification.

• Types of Fingerprints: Based on the ridge pattern, there are four types of fingerprints, along with their approximate percentage distribution:
1. Loop (67%)
2. Whorl (25%)
3. Arch (6–7%)
4. Composite/Compound (1–2%)

topics.name: What are the traits of identification for living person? (B.A-13, B.B-2)
topics.answer:
The traits or data points used for the identification of a living person include:
• Photograph
• Handwriting
• Gait
• Speech and voice
• Tricks of manner and habit
• Mental power and memory
• Education

topics.name: Tell the medicolegal importance of 21 years of age. (B.B-1)
topics.answer:
The medicolegal importance of 21 years of age is as follows:
• Marriage (Male): It is the minimum age for the marriage contract of a male.
• Majority: It is the age for attainment of majority for a person who was previously under lawful guardianship.
• Juvenile Offenders (Female): A female juvenile offender is not detained in a correctional school beyond the age of 21 years.

topics.name: Define identification. Absolute data of identification? (B.B-3, 9, 16)
topics.answer:
• Identification: This means the exact establishment of the individuality of a person (living or dead).
• Absolute Data of Identification (Surest Methods): The two absolute/surest methods for fixing the personality of an individual are fingerprint and DNA print.

topics.name: Mention the medicolegal importance of 15 years & 16 years of age. (B.B-4)
topics.answer:
• Medicolegal Importance of 15 Years of Age:
o Rape: Sexual intercourse, even with one’s own wife, below 15 years constitutes rape.
o Employment: A person completing 15 years is allowed to work in a factory as an adult if a doctor grants a fitness certificate.

• Medicolegal Importance of 16 Years of Age:
o Consent to Sexual Intercourse: A girl above 16 years of age can give valid consent for sexual intercourse irrespective of marriage ("sweet sixteen").
o Juvenile Offenders (Male): Male offenders under this age are juvenile offenders who may be sent to a Brostal school.

topics.name: Define trace evidence? Give some example. (B.B-5)
topics.answer:
Trace evidence is defined as something contaminated with other materials that provides tremendous help in medicolegal and other forensic investigations.

Examples of trace evidence include:
• Blood
• Semen
• Saliva
• Hair
• Fingerprints
• Weapons and tools
• Clothes and fibers
• Dust and dirt

topics.name: Why dactylography is regarded as an absolute method of identification? (B.B-5)
topics.answer:
Dactylography (fingerprint system) is regarded as an absolute method of identification for several reasons:
• Accuracy: It is a 100% accurate method in establishing identity.
• Uniqueness: It is a better method than even DNA profiling because DNA profiles would be the same in identical twins, but dactylography prints are different.
• Permanence: The fingerprint of an individual remains unaltered from birth to death.
• Accessibility: It is a cheap, easy, simple, and quicker method.

topics.name: How can you determine sex of a decomposed body? (B.B-11)
topics.answer:
The sex of a decomposed or mutilated body can be determined by examining the following:
• Bones: By studying the bones, particularly the pelvis, skull, and long bones.
• Internal Organs: The prostate and nulliparous uterus are the last organs to decompose and can help determine sex even in an advanced state of decomposition.
• Hair: The distribution of pubic hair can be examined (if present).
• Microscopic Study: Histological examination of residual tissue (if available) for sex chromatin (Barr bodies or Davidson bodies).
• DNA: DNA analysis from long bones or teeth.

topics.name: Define tattoo. What complication may arise? (B.B-12, 17)
topics.answer:
• Tattoo: A tattoo is defined as a design made on the skin artificially by multiple small punctures wound with needles or other pointed instruments dipped in coloring materials.
• Complications: Complications arising from tattooing include Abscess, AIDS, Allergic reaction, Erysipelas, Tetanus, Fungal infection, Hepatitis, Gangrene, Leprosy, and Tuberculosis.

topics.name: A headless dead body is brought... Determine height. (B.B-18)
topics.answer: The height (stature) of a headless dead body can be determined by applying forensic osteology techniques to the remaining parts, specifically the long bones. Experts examine and measure the long bones, such as the femur or humerus, to estimate the stature of the individual.

topics.name: Define scar. Is it temporary or permanent? (B.B-14, 19, 22)
topics.answer:
• Scar: A scar is a fibrous tissue covered by epithelium without hair follicles, sweat glands, and pigment produced from the healing of a wound.
• Permanence: Scars are permanent.

topics.name: What is race? Tell its types. (B.B-18, 21)
topics.answer:
• Race: Race is a biological concept denoting the different human species united by common heredity.
• Types (Major Human Races):
1. Caucasians (white)
2. Mongolian (yellow brown)
3. Negros (black)

topics.name: Mention medicolegal importance of Hair. (B.B-23)
topics.answer:
The medicolegal importance of hair includes:
• Identification: Hair is important in linking a weapon or an accused to a victim in criminal investigations.
• Wounds and Injuries: The nature of the weapon can sometimes be inferred from injuries to the hair, and hair findings help differentiate between scalds and burns. Singeing indicates burns or a close-range firearm injury.
• Poisoning: In cases of chronic poisoning involving heavy metals like arsenic, the poison can be detected in the hair.
• Age and Sex: Age can be roughly determined from hair growth/distribution, while sex can be determined from analysis for Barr bodies.
• Stains: Stains on the hair (such as mud, seminal fluid, or blood) may indicate the nature of the assault.

topics.name: What are the medicolegal importance of teeth? (B.B-25)
topics.answer:
The medicolegal importance of teeth (forensic odontology) includes:
• Identification: Dental identification is a highly sophisticated comparative method, useful in determining age, sex, and blood group (from pulp tissue). Identifying features like missing teeth or faulty alignment are crucial.
• Resistance to Decay: Teeth resist decomposition and fire, making them vital for identification in highly decomposed, mutilated, or burnt bodies.
• Injury Assessment: Loss of a tooth due to assault is legally considered a grievous hurt.
• Criminals/Bite Marks: Criminals can be identified through bite marks left on human tissue or foodstuffs. Bite marks found on private parts suggest sexual offense.
• Poisoning: Some poisons, such as arsenic and mercury, can be detected in the teeth even long after death.

topics.name: What are the information you will obtain from a sample of blood stain? (B.A-7)
topics.answer:
When examining a sample of blood stain, the following critical information can be obtained:
• Origin and Source: It is initially determined whether the stain is actually due to blood or some other material. If it is blood, forensic investigation ascertains if it is of human or animal origin. The examination also reveals the source of the bleeding, establishing if it belongs to the victim or the accused. Additionally, the source can be determined as being from an injury, haematemesis, haemoptysis, miscarriage, or menstruation.
• Individual Characteristics: Analysis can determine the sex and blood group of the person from whom the blood originated.
• Time and Condition: The time elapsed since the bleeding occurred (e.g., time of assault or injury) and whether the bleeding was antemortem or postmortem can be determined. Experts also look for abnormal constituents like fetal haemoglobin.

topics.name: What are the examinations you will do in a suspected case of seminal stain? (B.B-1)
topics.answer:
To detect and analyze a suspected case of seminal stain, several systematic examinations are performed:
• Physical Tests: This involves examining characteristics such as color, odor, consistency, appearance, and specific gravity of the stain.
• Microscopic Examination: This is conducted to gain absolute proof by detecting the presence of spermatozoa, which confirms the existence of semen.
• Chemical Tests: A variety of specific chemical analyses are used, including the Florence test, the Barbcrio's test, and tests focusing on acid phosphatase, creatine phosphokinase (CPK), choline, and spermine.
• Immunological Methods: These methods are also employed during the chemical examination process.

topics.name: An unknown body was found in the jungle. What samples will you collect for DNA profiling? (B.B-10, 16)
topics.answer:
For DNA profiling of an unknown dead body, samples are collected based on the best chance of obtaining viable DNA, regardless of the state of decomposition:
• Hard Tissues: The best materials for DNA analysis, particularly in decomposed bodies, are those that resist decay, such as long bones and teeth (specifically the 2nd molar teeth).
• Soft Tissues: Samples of soft tissues that can be collected include blood, buccal swabs, muscle, skin, and tissues from internal organs, including the brain.
• Appendages: Other useful specimens include hairs and finger nails.

topics.name: A blood stain was found in the scene of the crime. How will you determine whether it is ordinary blood or menstrual blood? (B.B-11, 17)
topics.answer:
The key differences between ordinary (fresh) blood and menstrual blood allow for their differentiation based on specific properties and contents:

| Trait | Fresh / Ordinary Blood | Menstrual Blood |
|---|---|---|
| Clot formation | Occurs. | Not occurs. |
| Color | Bright red. | Dark red. |
| Odour | Salty odour. | Disagreeable, fishy odour. |
| Reaction | Alkaline. | Acidic. |
| Viscosity | Less viscid. | More viscid. |
| Micro-organisms | Absent (except in disease). | Normally contains micro-organisms (e.g., lactobacilli). |
| Contents | Lacks endometrial and vaginal epithelial cells. | Contains endometrial and vaginal epithelial cells. |

topics.name: What is 'I', 'Y' and modified 'Y' shaped incision? (B.A-1, 4)
topics.answer:
These terms refer to the primary incisions used to open the neck, thorax, and abdomen during an autopsy:
• 'I' (Midline) shaped incision: This incision extends vertically down the midline, typically from the chin to the pubic symphysis, passing either to the left or right of the umbilicus.
• 'Y' shaped incision: This incision begins with two separate cuts originating from a point close to the acromial process of each clavicle. These two cuts extend downward below the breast to the xiphoid process, where they meet to form a single vertical line that is then carried down to the pubic symphysis.
• Modified 'Y' shaped incision: This may refer to the technique where a midline incision is made from the suprasternal notch to the symphysis pubis, and the incision then extends from the suprasternal notch over the clavicle to its center on both sides, passing upwards over the neck behind the ear.

topics.name: What preservatives are used during viscera preservation? (B.A-2, 4, 8, 18)
topics.answer:
The choice of preservative depends heavily on the substance being investigated:
• Saturated Sodium Chloride (NaCl) solution: This is a frequently used preservative because it is easily available, cheap, and effective. It is generally used except in suspected poisoning cases involving corrosive substances, phenol, alkali, corrosive sublimate, and aconite.
• Rectified spirit: This is typically used in cases where alkali or acid poisoning is suspected (with the exception of carbolic acid).
• Sodium or Potassium Fluoride and Potassium Oxalate: These agents are used for preserving blood intended for toxicological analysis (e.g., for alcohol, cocaine, cyanide, and carbon monoxide poisoning).
• 10% Neutral Formalin or 95% Alcohol: These are used for preserving tissues when the viscera are needed for histopathological examination.
• Formaldehyde should generally not be used for viscera preservation in poisoning cases.

topics.name: What are the incisions given during autopsy? (B.A-3)
topics.answer:
The major incisions given during a medicolegal autopsy involve the scalp, head, and trunk:
• Head/Scalp: A transverse incision is made across the vertex of the head, extending from one mastoid process to the other.
• Trunk (Neck, Thorax, and Abdomen): The body cavities are usually opened using either the 'I'-shaped incision or the 'Y'-shaped incision. A midline incision extending from the suprasternal notch to the symphysis pubis with lateral extensions over the clavicles may also be used.

topics.name: What are the different types of autopsy? (B.A-4, 23)
topics.answer:
Autopsies are primarily classified into three categories:
1. Medicolegal autopsy: This is performed in unnatural and suspicious circumstances of death and is compulsory by law. Its purpose is to determine the cause of death and aid in the administration of justice.
2. Pathological (clinical) autopsy: This is performed by a pathologist or clinician when the diagnosis could not be reached during treatment or to confirm a doubtful diagnosis.
3. Anatomical (academic) autopsy: This involves the dissection of dead bodies by students of anatomy for educational purposes to understand the details of organs and structures.

topics.name: What is negative autopsy? Tell the causes of negative autopsy. (B.A-5, 20)
topics.answer:
A negative autopsy occurs when gross and microscopic examination, toxicological analyses, and laboratory investigations fail to reveal a cause of death. This finding is reported in approximately 2–5% of all autopsies.

Causes of negative autopsy include:
• Inadequate or incorrect history (e.g., death due to vagal inhibition or status epilepticus may show no anatomical findings).
• Insufficient external examination (e.g., missing fresh or old needle marks in a drug addict, or overlooked burns in electrocution cases).
• Improper internal examination (e.g., missing air embolism or pneumothorax).
• Lack of toxicological analysis or insufficient laboratory investigation.
• Lack of adequate training of the doctor conducting the post-mortem.

topics.name: What is obscure autopsy? Give example/Causes. (B.A-6, 7, 16)
topics.answer:
Obscure autopsies are those that do not yield a definite cause of death because the findings are minimal, indefinite, or entirely absent.

Causes of obscure autopsy include:
• Natural diseases: Cases involving obscure or microscopic findings only, or death precipitated by emotional stress or functional failure (e.g., epilepsy).
• Biochemical disturbances: Such as Uraemia or Diabetes Mellitus (DM).
• Endocrine dysfunction: Including Adrenal insufficiency.
• Concealed trauma: Such as cerebral concussion or reflex vagal inhibition.
• Poisoning: Cases involving neurotoxic or cytotoxic poisons, or death due to anaesthetic overdose without macroscopic changes.
• Miscellaneous causes: Including drug idiosyncrasy or allergies.

topics.name: Name the viscera preserved in suspected poisoning. (B.A-8, 18)
topics.answer:
In cases of suspected poisoning, the following are generally preserved for chemical analysis:
• Stomach: The whole stomach with its entire contents.
• Intestine: About 15-30 cm of the upper part of the small intestine.
• Liver: Not less than 500g, or half of the liver (if the weight is less than 500g, the whole liver is preserved).
• Kidneys: The longitudinal half of both kidneys.
• Blood: Approximately 30 ml (minimum 10 ml).
• Urine: Approximately 30 ml (if available).
• Additional materials like heart, brain, bone, hair, nails, uterus, and vagina may be collected depending on the specific poison suspected.

topics.name: Define virtual autopsy. What are the disadvantages of it? (B.A-17)
topics.answer:
Virtual autopsy (Virtopsy) is a non-invasive technique used to examine dead bodies, typically employing a combination of CT and MR imaging to determine the cause of death. This process aims to document forensic evidence in a minimally invasive and observer-independent manner.

While the sources emphasize the advantages of new technologies like imaging, they note that the rapid pace of technological advancement presents critical challenges regarding cost, validation, and training of personnel. Furthermore, ethical and legal concerns regarding privacy and the handling of sensitive genetic data must be considered when integrating such innovations.

topics.name: What do you mean by open verdict? (B.A-17)
topics.answer: An open verdict is an outcome of an inquest that is done indefinitely. It is delivered when the cause of death cannot be established after the autopsy. This inquest could be reopened at any later date if further information becomes available.

topics.name: Define Forensic Pathology. (B.A-21)
topics.answer: Forensic pathology is defined as the branch of Forensic medicine that utilizes medical and pathological principles in determining the manner and causes of death. It is specifically involved in determining the cause and manner of death in circumstances that are unnatural, suspicious, unexplained, unexpected, sudden, and medically unattended.

topics.name: What are the pre-requisites needed for medicolegal autopsy? (B.A-24)
topics.answer:
For a medicolegal autopsy, which does not require consent from relatives, the necessary pre-requisites include:
• A Chalan (written requisition) from an authorized person, such as a Police officer, Magistrate, or Coroner.
• A copy of the Inquest report.
• Identification of the deceased by the accompanying police officer.
• The examination must be conducted by an authorized Government medical officer.
• An adequate Mortuary.
• Sufficient Light, preferably natural sunlight, although artificial light may be used in urgent circumstances.

topics.name: A bundle of bones has been brought... What information you have to be obtained? (B.B-4)
topics.answer:
From a piece or bundle of bones, the following essential information is obtained in a forensic investigation:
• Whether the bones belong to a human or animal.
• Whether the parts belong to one or more individuals.
• Sex.
• Age of death.
• Race.
• Stature (height) of the person.
• Identification of the person.
• Nature of injury.
• Time since death.

topics.name: What are the parts of an autopsy report? (B.A-19)
topics.answer:
An autopsy report is comprised of three main sections:
1. The 1st part / Preamble: This section contains identifying details such as the authority ordering the examination, the date, time, and place of examination, and the particulars (name, age, sex) of the victim. It also includes the identification of the body.
2. The 2nd part / The body of the report: This section details the observations and findings of the doctor, including a complete description of the external and internal examination of the body, and the nature, direction, site, and dimensions of any wounds.
3. Conclusion / opinion with signature: This section states the doctor’s final opinion regarding the cause of death based on the findings. The report must be comprehensive, honest, objective, and scientific.

topics.name: Why saturated solution of common salt is preferable over rectified spirit for preservation of viscera? (B.B-8, 14)
topics.answer:
Saturated solution of common salt (NaCl) is commonly used because it is easily available, cheap, and effective as a preservative.

Rectified spirit is not preferred as a general preservative because it is itself an alcohol. Using rectified spirit is contraindicated in suspected poisoning cases involving compounds such as alcohol, kerosene, chloral hydrate, formic acid, or formaldehyde. The presence of the preservative itself would interfere with the chemical detection of these specific poisons during laboratory analysis.

topics.name: Define sudden death? Tell the causes of it. (B.A-2, 4, 5, 16, 19)
topics.answer:
Sudden death is defined as death occurring when a person is not known to have been suffering from any dangerous disease, injury, or poisoning, and is found dead or dies within 24 hours after the onset of terminal symptoms. The incidence of sudden death is approximately 10% of all deaths.

The common causes of sudden death are grouped by the affected system:
• Cardiovascular Causes (45% - 50%): These include coronary atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction (MI), coronary thrombosis, stenosis/insufficiency of heart valves, congenital heart diseases, acute/constrictive pericarditis, and infective conditions of the heart.
• Respiratory Causes (15% - 23%): Examples include pulmonary embolism, massive haemoptysis (e.g., from pulmonary tuberculosis), fulminating viral infections or pneumonias, status asthmaticus, oedema glottis, or obstruction of the respiratory tract.
• CNS Causes (10% - 18%): These include intracranial haemorrhage, subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral thrombosis, meningitis, encephalitis, epilepsy, brain tumour, or brain abscess.
• Miscellaneous Causes (5% - 10%): These encompass conditions such as reflex vagal inhibition, anaphylactic reactions, or mismatched blood transfusion.

topics.name: Define death. Tell the types of death. (B.A-10, 17)
topics.answer:
Death is the permanent and irreversible cessation of functions of the 3 interlinked vital systems of the body, collectively known as the Bishop’s tripod of life. These three vital systems are the nervous, circulatory, and respiratory systems. A person may be pronounced dead if they cannot spontaneously pick up and survive after the withdrawal of artificial maintenance.

Death is primarily classified in the following ways:
• Clinical Stages of Death:
1. Somatic Death (Clinical/Systemic Death): The complete and irreversible stoppage of circulation, respiration, and brain function.
2. Molecular Death (Cellular Death): The death of cells and tissues individually, which occurs usually one to two hours after somatic death.
• Medicolegal Classification (Manner of Death):
1. Suicidal.
2. Homicidal.
3. Accidental.
4. Natural (due to disease).
• Gordon Classification (Hypoxia/Anoxia): This classification links death to four ways in which tissue anoxia occurs: Anoxic anoxia, Anaemic anoxia, Stagnant anoxia, and Histotoxic anoxia.

topics.name: What is Apparent death? What are the causes of it? (B.A-1)
topics.answer:
Apparent death (also called suspended animation or death trance) is the condition in which signs of life are not found because the functions of the nervous, circulatory, or respiratory systems are interrupted for some time, or are reduced to a minimum, although the person is still alive. The functions of these systems may subsequently return, either spontaneously or after resuscitation.

Apparent death may occur voluntarily (e.g., by practitioners of Yoga) or involuntarily in circumstances such as:
• Drowning.
• Electrocution.
• Severe syncopal attack.
• Newborn infants.
• Cerebral concussion or Sunstroke.
• Cholera, Narcotic poisoning, or Shock.

topics.name: Tell the difference between rigor mortis & cadaveric spasm. (B.A-3, 24)
topics.answer:
| Traits | Rigor Mortis (R.M.) | Cadaveric Spasm (C.S.) |
|---|---|---|
| Onset | Occurs 1-2 hours after death. | Occurs instantaneously at the moment of death. |
| Mechanism | Known; caused by the progressive and irreversible breakdown of ATP. | Not clearly known; possibly neurogenic. |
| Primary Flaccidity | R.M. follows the stage of primary muscular relaxation. | C.S. occurs without passing through the stage of primary relaxation. |
| Muscles Involved | Affects all muscles of the body (voluntary and involuntary) gradually. | Usually restricted to a single group of voluntary muscles. |
| Rigidity | Comparatively moderate stiffness. | Marked stiffness (great force is required to overcome it). |
| Medicolegal Value | Used primarily to estimate the time since death. | Reflects the deceased's last act, often grasping a weapon or object. |

topics.name: What is rigor mortis? Tell its mechanism. (B.A-6)
topics.answer:
Rigor mortis is a condition characterized by the stiffening and shortening of the muscles that occurs due to chemical changes within their myofibrils following the period of primary relaxation after death.

Mechanism of Rigor Mortis: Rigor mortis is a physicochemical change that results from a lack of oxygen after death, leading to the cessation of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) production.
1. ATP Depletion: Energy cannot be produced from glycogen via glucose using oxidative phosphorylation, and remaining ATP is progressively destroyed.
2. Actinomyosin Link: When ATP levels fall to a critical level (85% of normal), the actin and myosin fibers bind together, forming a rigid link known as actinomyosin.
3. Muscle Stiffness: Since energy (ATP) is required to separate the actin and myosin filaments, the lack of ATP causes the muscles to become hard and rigid. Maximum rigidity occurs when ATP falls to 15% of normal.

topics.name: Tell the difference between lung findings of live birth and stillbirth. (B.A-9)
topics.answer:
The following differences in post-mortem findings of the lungs are observed when comparing stillbirth (un-respired lungs) and live birth (respired lungs):

| Traits | Un-respired Lungs (Stillbirth) | Respired Lungs (Live Birth) |
|---|---|---|
| Weight | 1/70 of body weight. | 1/35 of body weight. |
| Volume | Normal or small. | Larger and cover the heart. |
| Consistency | Dense, firm, non-crepitant. | Soft, spongy, crepitant (due to air). |
| Color | Uniformly reddish-brown or bluish-red. | Mottled or marbled appearance. |
| Flotation (Hydrostatic Test) | Whole and parts sink in water. | Expanded areas or whole float in water. |

topics.name: Define rigor mortis. Tell the conditions simulating rigor mortis. (B.A-12)
topics.answer:
Rigor mortis is a state characterized by the stiffening & shortening of the muscle that results from chemical changes in the muscle fibers following the initial period of primary relaxation after death.

The conditions that may resemble or simulate true rigor mortis (the differential diagnoses) include:
1. Cadaveric spasm (Instantaneous rigor).
2. Heat stiffening.
3. Cold stiffening.
4. Gas stiffening (due to putrefactive gases).

topics.name: Tell the difference between antemortem and postmortem clot. (B.A-12, 23)
topics.answer:
| Points | Ante-mortem clot | Post-mortem clot |
|---|---|---|
| Color | Yellow or grayish white. | Dark red or almost black. |
| Consistency | Firm. | Gelatinous. |
| Elasticity | Elastic. | Non-elastic. |
| Composition | Formed mainly by platelet & fibrin. | Characterized by a lack of fibrin strands. |
| Attachment | Attached to the vessel wall. | Not attached to the vessel wall. |

topics.name: What do you mean by post mortem caloricity? (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
Post-mortem caloricity is a phenomenon where the temperature of the dead body, instead of immediately dropping (cooling), remains raised for the first two hours following death.

This condition occurs in certain cases due to underlying pathological states that cause excessive heat generation just prior to or at the time of death, including:
• Sun stroke or heat hyperpyrexia.
• Septicaemic conditions.
• Tetanus or strychnine poisoning.
• Febrile illness due to other causes.

topics.name: Define death trance. Give some examples. (B.A-11, 16)
topics.answer:
Death trance (suspended animation or apparent death) is the state in which signs of life (nervous, circulatory, respiratory) are so reduced to a minimum that they cannot be perceived by conventional methods, although the person is still alive.

Involuntary examples of conditions where death trance may be observed include:
• Drowning.
• Electrocution.
• Severe syncopal attack.
• Newborn infants.
• Cerebral concussion or Sunstroke.
• Narcotic poisoning.

topics.name: Define molecular death. Tell its MLI. (B.A-21)
topics.answer:
Molecular death (or cellular death) is the death of individual cells and tissues, a process that occurs, usually, one to two hours after the cessation of the vital functions (somatic death).

Medicolegal Importance (MLI):
1. It is considered a confirm sign of death.
2. It indicates the molecular death of the muscle involved.
3. Organ transplantation is not possible after molecular death has occurred.

topics.name: Tell the differences between Bruise and Hypostasis. (B.A-22)
topics.answer:
| Traits | Bruise (Contusion) | Hypostasis (Post-mortem Staining) |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Extravasation of blood due to rupture of vessels. | Capillo-venous distension due to gravity settling blood. |
| Nature of Change | Ante-mortem phenomenon. | Post-mortem phenomenon. |
| Effect of Pressure | No change on application of pressure. | Pressed spot appears pale. |
| Cut section | Haemorrhage evident; cannot be washed out. | Blood oozes out; can be cleaned by washing. |

topics.name: Define Forensic Thanatology. (B.A-24)
topics.answer: Forensic thanatology is the branch of forensic medicine concerned with the scientific study of death, which includes the types of death, the various events or changes that occur in the cadaver, and their medicolegal significance.

topics.name: Define mode of death. What are the modes of death? (B.A-20)
topics.answer:
The mode of death refers to the abnormal physiological state that existed at the time of death.

Based on the three vital systems (Bichat’s tripod of life), the modes of death are:
1. Coma: Failure of function of the brain.
2. Syncope: Failure of function of the heart.
3. Asphyxia: Failure of function of the respiratory system.

topics.name: Define Brain death. Tell its types. (B.A-15, 22)
topics.answer:
Brain death is defined as the permanent and irreversible cessation of function of the entire brain (cerebrum, cerebellum, and brain stem), even if the heart function is artificially maintained. This is considered legal death.

The sources define brain death based on the site of destruction in the brain:
1. Cortical or Cerebral Death (or Persistent Vegetative State): Only the cerebral cortex is destroyed, leaving the brain stem intact, which maintains continuous heartbeat and spontaneous respiration.
2. Brain Stem Death: Characterized by the loss of the vital respiratory center and ascending reticular activating system, leading to irreversible coma and inability to breathe spontaneously.
3. Whole Brain Death: Permanent cessation of functions of the cerebrum, cerebellum, and brain stem.

topics.name: Define Assault, Hurt and Battery? (B.A-3)
topics.answer:
• Assault: An assault is an offer or threat or attempt to apply force to the body of another in a hostile manner. It is a threat by one to the unlawful force against another.
• Hurt: Hurt means bodily pain, disease, or infirmity caused to any person. Inflicted mental pain is not included in this definition.
• Battery: Battery is intentional and unauthorized physical contact with a person without his consent. For example, a battery includes touching a person or laying hold on his clothes in a rude or hostile manner.

topics.name: Define Injury, Wound and Battery. (B.A-2, 3, 4)
topics.answer:
• Injury: Injury is any harm, whatever illegally caused to any person in body, mind, reputation, or property.
• Wound: A wound is a break of the natural continuity of any of the tissues of the living body. Surgically, it means breaking of the natural continuity of skin or mucous membrane.
• Battery: Battery is intentional and unauthorized physical contact with a person without his consent.

topics.name: Define bruise. Classify it. (B.A-15)
topics.answer:
• Definition: Bruises (or contusions/ecchymosis) are blunt force injuries characterized by extravasation of blood (hemorrhage) into the tissue spaces, typically caused by the rupture of capillaries. There is no breach of continuity of the overlying skin.
• Classification: Depending upon its location within the body, a bruise is classified as:
1. Intradermal.
2. Subcutaneous.
3. Deep.

topics.name: Define abrasion? Mention its types. (B.A-3, 8)
topics.answer:
• Definition: An abrasion is a superficial injury causing destruction of the skin, usually involving only the superficial layers of the epidermis. It results from friction or pressure against a hard, blunt, and rough surface.
• Types: Abrasions are generally classified into four types:
1. Scratches or linear abrasions (caused by pointed objects like fingernails or thorns).
2. Grazes or sliding abrasions (caused by movement between the skin and a rough surface, common in road traffic accidents).
3. Pressure abrasions (caused by crushing, such as the ligature mark in hanging or strangulation).
4. Impact abrasions (caused by striking against a rough object, often leaving a patterned imprint).

topics.name: Define laceration? What are types of it? (B.A-14)
topics.answer:
• Definition: A lacerated wound is a tear or split of the skin, mucous membrane, or underlying tissues caused by the application of blunt force, stretching the tissue beyond its elasticity. The wound margins are typically ragged and irregular.
• Types: Lacerated wounds are commonly divided into five types based on their production:
1. Split laceration (or incised-looking wound): Occurs when skin and subcutaneous tissue are crushed against bone by a blunt weapon, producing sharp, linear margins.
2. Stretch laceration: Caused by heavy, forceful frictional impact.
3. Avulsion (or shearing laceration): Caused by grinding compression that separates the skin from the underlying tissue.
4. Cut laceration: Produced by the "not-so-sharp" edge of a heavy cutting weapon, often seen in chop wounds.
5. Tear laceration: Tearing of skin and tissues that can occur from impact by an irregular or sharp object.

topics.name: What is incised wound? How is it produced? (B.A-17, 23)
topics.answer:
• Definition: An incised wound is a clean cut or slash produced by the sharp edge of a weapon such as a knife, dagger, or razor. Its length is typically greater than its depth and width.
• Production: The wound is caused by the striking, sawing, or drawing of the sharp edge upon the body. The margins of the resulting wound are characteristically well-defined, cleanly cut, and sharp.

topics.name: Tell the difference between antemortem wound and postmortem wound. (B.A-20)
topics.answer:
The difference between wounds inflicted during life (ante-mortem) and after death (post-mortem) primarily rests on the presence of vital reactions:

| Trait | Ante-mortem Wounds | Post-mortem Wounds |
|---|---|---|
| Edges | Swollen, everted, retracted, and the wound gapes. | Edges do not gape but are closely approximated. |
| Hemorrhage | Abundant and usually arterial. | Slight or none, and always venous. |
| Clotting | Clot is present and firmly attached. | No clotting or soft clot. |
| Inflammation/Repair | Present (vital reaction). | Absent. |
| Color | Bright-reddish brown. | Yellowish. |

topics.name: Tell the components of Grievous hurt? (B.A-10, 12)
topics.answer:
Grievous hurt refers to specific severe injuries listed in Section 320 BPC. The eight components are:
1. Emasculation.
2. Permanent privation of sight of either eye.
3. Permanent privation of hearing of either ear.
4. Privation of any member or joint.
5. Destruction or permanent impairing of the power of any member or joint.
6. Permanent disfiguration of head or face.
7. Fracture or dislocation of a bone or teeth.
8. Any hurt which endangers life, or which causes the sufferer severe bodily pain, or makes him unable to follow his ordinary pursuits for a period of 20 days.

topics.name: How will you diagnose the age of this wound (Blue/Brown Bruise)? (B.A-6, 7, 24)
topics.answer:
The age of a bruise is determined by the color changes resulting from the breakdown of hemoglobin:
• Blue Bruise (Few hours – 3 days): The color turns blue due to the process of deoxygenated hemoglobin formation.
• Brown Bruise (4th day): The color becomes bluish-black to brown due to the conversion of hemoglobin into hemosiderin.

topics.name: Incised wound more dangerous than incised-looking wound - explain. (B.A-16, 21)
topics.answer:
An incised wound is generally considered more dangerous than an incised-looking wound (split laceration) primarily because of the mechanism of bleeding:
1. Cause and Margins: An incised wound is caused by a sharp cutting weapon with cleanly cut margins. An incised-looking wound is caused by blunt force applied over a bony prominence, resulting in splitting of the skin.
2. Hemorrhage: In an incised wound, blood vessels are cleanly cut, leading to delayed clotting and profuse external bleeding. This excessive blood loss is a common cause of death (hemorrhagic shock). Conversely, in an incised-looking wound, the vessels are crushed or torn across, promoting rapid clotting and resulting in less pronounced bleeding.

topics.name: Case: Dislocated upper 2 incisor teeth. Type of injury? (B.A-9)
topics.answer: The injury is a Grievous Hurt. Dislocation of a tooth is one of the specific components listed under the definition of grievous hurt in the Penal Code.

topics.name: Case: Fractured nasal bone after punch. (B.A-16)
topics.answer: The injury is a Grievous Hurt. A fracture of a bone (nasal bone) is defined as one of the components of grievous hurt.

topics.name: Case: 2 stab injuries over abdomen. (B.A-20)
topics.answer:
• Cause of Death: The major immediate cause of death from stab injuries over the abdomen is typically Hemorrhage leading to shock, resulting from injury to internal vessels or organs. Delayed causes include infection leading to peritonitis or septic shock.
• Weapon Identification: The external and internal appearance of the wounds must be examined. A doctor should look for the dimensions of the wound, the shape (e.g., elliptical/slit-like for a double-edged weapon or triangular/wedge-shaped for a single-edged weapon), the taper of the blade, and the depth of the wound.

topics.name: How will you prepare an injury certificate? (B.A-19)
topics.answer:
The injury certificate (or report) is typically prepared on a printed form, with one copy sent to the investigating officer and one retained. The report must include the following particulars:
1. Preamble/Particulars: Serial number, the name, age, sex, and address of the injured person. The name of the accompanying police constable must also be recorded.
2. History: A brief statement from the injured person detailing how the injury was sustained.
3. Examination and Description: The height, weight, and general health of the victim. At least two identification marks should be noted. The written consent of the patient for the examination must be taken.
4. Injury Details (Specific Entries): A detailed description of all wounds, including their size, shape, direction, the part of the body afflicted, and their nature (simple or grievous).
5. Weapon/Circumstance: The kind of weapon inflicted and whether it was dangerous should be noted.
6. Conclusion/Opinion: The doctor’s final opinion or inference regarding the injuries, followed by the signature and date.

topics.name: What do you mean by lucid interval of head injury? (B.A-2, 14, B.B-7)
topics.answer: A lucid interval is a temporary period of improvement in a patient's condition following a traumatic brain injury, after which the patient's condition deteriorates. It occurs after the initial concussive force momentarily knocks the patient out or dazes them, and they then become relatively lucid for a period that may last minutes or hours. This period of lucidity is followed by a rapid decline as blood collects within the skull, causing intracranial pressure to rise and damage to the brain tissue. A lucid interval is especially indicative of an epidural haematoma, although only 20% to 50% of patients with an epidural hematoma experience this phenomenon.

topics.name: What do you mean by cerebral concussion? (B.A-18, 21)
topics.answer: Cerebral concussion is defined as a 'jar' or 'shock' to the brain. The condition is also known by the older term Commotio cerebri.

topics.name: What do you mean by whiplash injury? (B.A-25)
topics.answer: Whiplash injury is a form of injury to the spine that occurs without a fracture of the vertebral column. It is commonly sustained by occupants of the front seat of a motor vehicle when the vehicle comes to a sudden stop. The resulting forward thrust causes acute hyperflexion, which is rapidly converted into acute hyperextension when the forehead strikes the windscreen. This movement can cause fatal contusion or laceration of the spinal cord even if the spine itself is not fractured.

topics.name: Moving head produces contrecoup injury - explain it. (B.A-17)
topics.answer: Contrecoup injury refers to a lesion that occurs in an area of the brain or skull opposite to the side of impact. This type of injury is caused when the moving head is suddenly decelerated by striking a firm surface, such as when the head hits the ground during a fall. The commonest cause of contrecoup injury is a Road Traffic Accident (RTA). The sudden deceleration causes the brain to strike the inside of the skull opposite to the site of impact, resulting in lesions such as sub-dural or sub-arachnoid hemorrhage.

topics.name: What is brain death? Tell its types. (B.A-15, 22)
topics.answer:
Brain death is defined as the permanent and irreversible cessation of function of the entire brain (cerebrum, cerebellum, and brain stem). This condition is considered legal death, even if the heart function is artificially maintained by mechanical means.

The types of brain death are classified based on the site of destruction:
1. Cortical or Cerebral Death (Persistent Vegetative State): In this condition, only the cerebral cortex is destroyed, but the brain stem remains intact. Respiration and continuous heartbeat are maintained, but there is a total loss of the power of perception.
2. Brain Stem Death: This is characterized by the loss of the vital respiratory center and the ascending reticular activating system in the brain stem. This leads to irreversible coma and the inability to breathe spontaneously.
3. Whole Brain Death: This involves the permanent cessation of function across all parts of the brain, including the cerebrum, cerebellum, and brain stem.

topics.name: Tell the difference between Exit wound and Entry wound in firearm injury. (B.A-2, 6, 7)
topics.answer:
The characteristics of entrance and exit wounds produced by a bullet from a rifled firearm differ based on the interaction of the missile with the body tissues:

| Trait | Entrance Wound | Exit Wound |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Smaller than the diameter of the bullet owing to the elasticity of the skin. | Bigger than the diameter of the bullet. |
| Edges | Inverted. | Everted, puckered, torn. |
| Collars/Residue | Bruising, abrasion, or grease collar might be found. Burning, blackening, or tattooing may be seen around the wound. | Absent. |
| Bleeding | Less. | More. |
| Tissue Changes | May be cherry red due to Carbon Monoxide of explosive gases. | No color changes. |
| Clothing Fibers | Turned in. | Turned out. |

topics.name: What is 'choking' of firearm? Tell its importance. (B.A-5, 16)
topics.answer:
Choking of the barrel is the slight constriction of the terminal few centimeters of the bore near the muzzle end of some smooth-bored guns.

The importance and advantages of choking are:
• It keeps the pellets compact and prevents their dispersion up to a longer distance.
• The pellets act like a single ball, which exerts a greater impact at the site of the body struck, leading to a larger entry wound with more penetrating power.
• It temporarily obstructs the expulsion of the pellets, increasing the gas pressure behind them, which causes them to be expelled with greater force, thus increasing their effective range and penetration capacity.

topics.name: Brief characteristic points of entrance wound of rifled firearm. (B.A-3)
topics.answer:
The characteristic points of an entrance wound caused by a rifled firearm (bullet) are:
• The wound is typically smaller than the diameter of the bullet due to the elasticity of the skin.
• The edges are inverted.
• The wound may be surrounded by a bruising, abrasion, or grease collar.
• Depending on the range of fire, burning, blackening, or tattooing may be found around the wound.
• Fibers of clothing, if present, are typically turned in.
• The tissues within and around the wound may be cherry red due to the Carbon Monoxide (CO) present in the explosive gases.

topics.name: What is rifling? What are the advantages of it? (B.A-11)
topics.answer:
Rifling is the grooving on the inner surface of the barrel of a firearm. These grooves run spirally all along the bore.

The advantages and significance of rifling are:
• It helps the bullet achieve a gyroscopic movement (spin).
• This spin gives the bullet a straight trajectory.
• It imparts greater power of penetration to the bullet.
• It enables the bullet to cover longer ranges and improves the accuracy of hitting the target.

topics.name: What is bomb? What are the effects of a bomb blast? (B.A-10, 17, 23)
topics.answer:
A bomb is defined as a container filled with an explosive mixture and missiles, which is fired either by a detonator or a fuse.

The effects of a bomb blast explosion include:
• Disruptive effects: Victims in contact with a large bomb may be blown into pieces. A bomb exploding on the ground may cause traumatic amputation of the lower legs.
• Effect of flying missiles (splinters): Splinters from the bomb can cause deep penetrating injuries, injury to vital organs, hemorrhage, shock, and potentially death. Secondary blast injuries caused by small missiles striking the body are characterized by the Marshal Triad (punctate bruises, abrasions, and punctate lacerations).
• Effect of air blast: The explosion produces a shock wave that can throw the victim against a wall or toss them through the air, causing blunt force injuries. Death may result from blast lung.
• Burns: The fire caused by the explosion may set the victim's clothing ablaze, resulting in extensive burn injury.
• Falling masonry: A powerful blast can cause the collapse of a structure, leading to traumatic asphyxia.
• Fumes: If the explosion occurs inside a closed apartment, the entrapped victims may suffer poisoning with CO2 or CO, leading to death by asphyxia.

topics.name: Define Forensic Ballistics. (B.A-20)
topics.answer: Forensic ballistics is the science that deals with the investigations of firearms, ammunition, and the problems arising from their use.

topics.name: What is primer? What are the composition of it? (B.A-25)
topics.answer:
The primer, also known as priming mixture, is the highly explosive mixture found in the percussion cap at the base of the cartridge.

The composition of the primer includes Mercury fulminate, Potassium carbonate (KCO3), and Antimony sulfide.

topics.name: What is souvenir bullet? Tell its medicolegal importance. (B.A-13)
topics.answer:
A souvenir bullet is a bullet that remains in the body for a long time. It is typically surrounded by a dense fibrous tissue capsule, and there is no fresh bleeding in the surrounding areas.

The medicolegal importance is that lead poisoning may occur due to the absorption of lead from the bullet remaining embedded in the tissues.

topics.name: Case: Firearm injury, single entry wound & two exit wounds. Cause? (B.A-1)
topics.answer:
A single entrance wound resulting in two or more exit wounds can be caused if:
• The bullet splits up within the body and divides into two or more pieces.
• The bullet strikes a bone, breaking it into fragments that then act as secondary missiles producing multiple exits.

topics.name: Case: Entry wound but no exit wound and no bullet found. Cause? (B.A-4)
topics.answer:
If an entry wound is present but no exit wound and no bullet are found inside the body, the cause could be:
• The bullet entered the stomach and was subsequently vomited.
• The bullet entered the windpipe and was coughed up.
• The bullet entered the gastrointestinal tract and passed out in the feces.
• The bullet was turned on coming in contact with the bone and passed out by the same wound through which it entered.

topics.name: Case: Circular hole right temporal, pistol in hand. Suicide or Homicide? (B.A-10)
topics.answer:
This scenario is strongly suggestive of suicide.

Factors supporting this opinion include:
• The wound site (temporal region) is a common suicidal site.
• The pistol is found at the victim's hand, which, especially if grasped in cadaveric spasm, is strong evidence of suicide.
• Most suicidal wounds are contact wounds.
• In such a case, a contact shot by a pistol results in a larger, stellate-like entry wound at the site of impact.
• The scene is usually undisturbed and the victim is typically found in a closed room.

topics.name: Case: Multiple entry/exit wounds by single bullet. (B.A-15)
topics.answer:
Multiple entry and exit wounds (more than one entry and one exit) caused by a single bullet occur if:
• The bullet passes through two or more separate parts of the body.
• For instance, a single bullet passing through the arm and then the chest would create four wounds (two entry, two exit).
• This usually happens when the person is running or sitting in an unusual position.

topics.name: Case: 2 gunshot injuries (front and back of chest). Diagnose entry/exit. (B.A-19, 21)
topics.answer:
Assuming a frontal attack, the wound in the front of the chest is typically the entry wound and the one at the back is the exit wound.

Entry Wound (Front of chest):
• Size: Smaller than the bullet diameter.
• Edges: Inverted.
• Residue: May show burning, blackening, tattooing, abrasion, or grease collars.

Exit Wound (Back of chest):
• Size: Bigger than the bullet diameter.
• Edges: Everted, puckered, or torn.
• Residue: Absent.

topics.name: Case: Dead body gunshot chest. Rifled or Smooth bored? (B.A-23)
topics.answer:
To determine whether the injury was caused by a rifled firearm (bullet) or a smooth bored firearm (shotgun), the following must be investigated:
• Rifled Firearm: Typically produces a single, circular or oval entry wound, and if discharged from close range, leaves behind a single bullet.
• Smooth Bored Firearm: Shoots pellets and:
o At long range, the injury shows individual pellet holes.
o At intermediate range, it forms a ragged central hole surrounded by a few stray shot holes.
o An X-ray would likely show the presence of multiple pellets (shot).

The definitive diagnosis relies on the distinct pattern of the wound (single versus pellet dispersal) and the presence of any residual projectiles.

data.title: Effects of Heat, Cold, Electrocution & Starvation

topics.name: Differentiate between burn & scald. (B.A-1, 4)
topics.answer:
Burn and scald injuries, classified as thermal injuries, differ primarily based on the nature of the heat source.

| Trait | Burn (Dry Heat Injury) | Scald (Moist Heat Injury) |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Dry heat, flame, heated solid body, or X-rays. | Steam or liquid above 60°C. |
| Skin Appearance | Dry and wrinkled; may be charred or blackened. | Sodden and bleached. |
| Singeing of Hair | Usually present. | Usually absent. |
| Depth/Severity | Often deep, potentially causing tissue destruction/charring. | Usually less deep. |
| Scar | Thick and contracted. | Thin and less contracted. |
| Fatality | More fatal. | Less fatal. |

topics.name: 'AC current is more dangerous than DC current'- why? (B.A-1)
topics.answer: AC causes tetanic muscle contraction (cannot let go) and ventricular fibrillation.

topics.name: How will you differentiate between antemortem burn and postmortem burn? (B.A-5, 7, 15, 16)
topics.answer:
Antemortem (during life) and postmortem (after death) burns are differentiated based on the presence of vital reactions (biological changes only possible in a living body).

| Trait | Ante-mortem Burn | Post-mortem Burn |
|---|---|---|
| Line of Redness | Present (due to vital reaction). | Absent. |
| Blister Base | Red and inflamed. | Dry, hard, and yellow. |
| Blister Contents | Contains serous fluid with proteins and chlorides. | Contains air and thin clear fluid. |
| Vital Reaction/Enzymes | Present; peripheral zone shows increase in enzyme reaction. | Absent; peripheral zone does not show increase in enzyme reaction. |
| Carboxy-Hb in Blood | Present (if combustion products were inhaled). | Absent. |

topics.name: What do you mean by post mortem caloricity? (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
Post-mortem caloricity is a phenomenon in which the temperature of the dead body remains raised for the first two hours or shortly after death, instead of immediately beginning to cool.

This condition is seen in cases where excessive heat generation occurs just prior to or at the time of death, such as in:
• Sun stroke or heat hyperpyrexia.
• Tetanus and strychnine poisoning.
• Septicaemic conditions.
• Febrile illness due to other causes.

topics.name: What is sudden death? Tell the cardiovascular causes. (B.A-2, 5, 7, 19)
topics.answer:
Sudden death is defined as death occurring when a person is not known to have been suffering from any dangerous disease, injury, or poisoning, and is found dead or dies within 24 hours after the onset of terminal symptoms.

Cardiovascular causes account for 45% to 50% of sudden deaths. These causes include:
• Coronary atherosclerosis or coronary artery disease.
• Coronary thrombosis and myocardial infarction.
• Ruptured myocardial infarct.
• Stenosis or insufficiency of heart valves.
• Pulmonary embolism.
• Congenital heart diseases.
• Acute and constrictive pericarditis or myocarditis.
• Cardiomyopathy.

topics.name: Case: Emaciated body, distended gall bladder. Cause? (B.A-2, 8, 12, 17, 20)
topics.answer:
The diagnosis consistent with an emaciated body and a distended gallbladder is Chronic Starvation.

The gall bladder is found distended with thick, inspissated bile in starvation cases. This occurs because the absence of food in the stomach leads to no stimulation of nerve receptors normally responsible for releasing bile from the gallbladder. Consequently, the organ remains distended with concentrated bile.

topics.name: Case: Burn 30% (or upper limbs/chest). Fluid management? (B.A-8, 12)
topics.answer:
The fluid management for a burn patient is based on the Parkland formula, which calculates the amount of Ringer’s lactate (crystalloid solution) needed in the first 24 hours.

The formula calculates the total 24-hour fluid requirement as: Total Fluid (mL) = 4 × Body Weight (kg) × % Total Burn Surface Area

This calculated volume is administered over the first 24 hours post-burn in the following regimen:
