• First 8 hours: 50% of the total calculated fluid amount.
• Next 8 hours: 25% of the total calculated fluid amount.
• Remaining 8 hours (total 24 hours): 25% of the total calculated fluid amount.

topics.name: Case: Farmer, hot day, unconscious, high temp. Diagnosis? (B.A-18, 24)
topics.answer:
The probable diagnosis is Heat Stroke (Heat hyperpyrexia/Sun Stroke).

Heat stroke is characterized by the following critical features:
• High Temperature: The body temperature is elevated, often greater than 41°C (106°F).
• Unconsciousness/Neurological Disturbance: The victim presents with neurological disturbances such as stupor or coma.
• Mechanism: It results from the impairment or breakdown of the heat regulation mechanism, which causes the heat regulation centers to fail. The skin is hot and dry with no sweating.

topics.name: Case: 2 patients burn injuries. One restless, one quiet. Reason? (B.A-25)
topics.answer:
The difference in patient behavior is related to the depth of the burn injury and the destruction of local nerve endings.

1. Restless/Screaming Patient (Painful): This suggests a superficial burn (1st or 2nd degree) where the nerve endings remain functional but are severely irritated. This type of burn is the most painful.
2. Calm/Quiet Patient (Painless): This suggests a deep burn (3rd degree or deeper) where the nerve endings are completely destroyed by the heat, rendering the area relatively painless.

topics.name: Tell the differential diagnosis of starvation. (B.A-24, 25)
topics.answer:
The diagnosis of starvation requires the exclusion of natural diseases that cause severe weight loss or cachexia. Differential diagnoses of chronic starvation include:
• Malignant diseases.
• Pulmonary TB (Tuberculosis).
• Diabetes mellitus (DM).
• Addison's disease.
• Thyrotoxicosis.
• Chronic diarrhea/Gastroenteritis.
• Progressive muscular atrophy.
• Pernicious anaemia.
• Progressive muscular dystrophy.

topics.name: What is asphyxia? Tell the cardinal signs of asphyxia. (B.A-10, 11)
topics.answer:
Asphyxia is defined as the condition caused by interference with respiration, or due to a lack of oxygen in the inspired air, resulting in the appreciable reduction of oxygen supply to the blood and tissues below the normal working level. This deprivation of oxygen, along with the failure to eliminate carbon dioxide, leads to unconsciousness or death. The term originally means "absence of pulse".

The cardinal signs (or stigmata) of asphyxial death are primarily due to raised venous pressure and include:
1. Cyanosis, resulting from deoxygenated blood.
2. Congestion, caused by stasis.
3. Petechial haemorrhage (or Tardieu’s spots), caused by the rupture of small venules.
4. Interstitial edema, also resulting from stasis.

topics.name: Define Strangulation. Classify it. (B.A-9, 25)
topics.answer:
Strangulation is a form of violent asphyxia caused by constriction of the neck by a ligature or by other mechanical means, where the constricting force is anything other than the weight of the body. The force of constriction is applied externally (exogenous in origin).

Strangulation can be classified by method:
1. Strangulation proper (Ligature strangulation).
2. Manual strangulation (Throttling, using the palm or fingers).
3. Bansdola (compression of the neck between two sticks or hard objects).
4. Mugging (compression of the neck against the bend of the elbow).
5. Garroting (using a rapidly twisted ligature with a lever).

topics.name: What are the criteria of ligature mark in case of hanging? (B.A-7)
topics.answer:
The typical ligature mark resulting from hanging has the following criteria:
• Oblique and Non-continuous: The mark is oblique and does not completely encircle the neck, with a gap present at the site of the knot.
• Location: It is typically placed high up around the neck, often above the level of the thyroid cartilage.
• Appearance: The base of the mark is usually pale, hard, and parchment-like. If the knot presses on the skin, it may be inverted ‘V’-shaped.

topics.name: What are the neck findings of hanging and strangulation? (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
The neck findings differ significantly based on the mechanism:

| Traits | Hanging (Typical Suicidal) | Strangulation (Ligature) |
|---|---|---|
| Ligature Mark | Oblique, non-continuous, high up. Base is pale and parchment-like. | Transverse, continuous, typically below the thyroid cartilage. Base is soft and reddish. |
| Neck Status | Stretched and elongated. | Not stretched or elongated. |
| Subcutaneous Tissue | Dry, firm, white, and glistening (parchment-like) under the mark. | Ecchymosed (bruised) under the mark. |
| Hyoid/Thyroid | Hyoid bone fracture may occur. Thyroid cartilage fracture is less common. | Thyroid cartilage fracture is more common. Hyoid fracture is uncommon. |
| Face Appearance | Usually pale. | Congested, livid, and marked with petechiae. |

topics.name: What is homicidal hanging? Give examples. (B.A-20)
topics.answer:
Homicidal hanging is extremely rare because it is very difficult for a single assailant to perform unless the victim is a child, very weak, or has been rendered unconscious by injury or drugs.

Homicide should be suspected if the body shows signs of struggle or violence, if the clothing is torn, or if injuries are present elsewhere on the body.

An example of homicidal hanging is Lynching, which occurs when several persons acting jointly and illegally overpower and hang an individual (often with a rope from a tree or similar object).

topics.name: Define throttling. Tell its MLI. (B.A-22)
topics.answer:
Throttling (Manual Strangulation) is defined as a form of strangulation caused by compressing the front of the neck by human hands (palm or fingers).

The Medicolegal Importance (MLI) of throttling is that it is always homicidal. It is the most common method used to commit homicide against weak individuals, children, or intoxicated persons. Death is caused primarily by asphyxia or sudden cardiac arrest due to reflex vagal inhibition caused by pressure on the vagal sheath or carotid vessels.

topics.name: Define partial hanging. Tell its types. (B.A-16)
topics.answer:
Partial (incomplete) hanging is a form of violent asphyxia where the lower part of the body is in touch with the ground. The constricting force is applied by the weight of the head alone (approximately 5 to 6 kg).

Partial hanging is classified by the victim's posture:
1. Standing hanging: Toes or feet are in touch with the ground.
2. Kneeling hanging: Knees are in touch with the ground.
3. Sitting hanging: Buttock is in touch with the ground.
4. Lying hanging (Prone or supine position).

topics.name: Define drowning. Tell its types. (B.A-21, 23)
topics.answer:
Drowning is a form of asphyxia that occurs when the entry of air into the lungs is prevented by water or other fluid due to submersion of the mouth and nostrils. Complete submersion of the body is not required.

Drowning is categorized into four main types:
1. Wet/Typical Drowning: Water is inhaled into the lungs. (Subtypes: Fresh water drowning and Salt water drowning).
2. Dry Drowning: Water does not enter the lungs; death is caused by immediate sustained laryngeal spasm.
3. Secondary Drowning (Near drowning): Death occurs hours to days after initial rescue and resuscitation.
4. Immersion Syndrome (Hydrocution): Death results from cardiac arrest due to vagal inhibition, often triggered by sudden immersion in cold water.

topics.name: What do you mean by suffocation? (B.A-15)
topics.answer:
Suffocation is a general term used to indicate a form of asphyxia that results from the deprivation of oxygen, either due to a lack of oxygen in the environment or caused by mechanical obstruction of the air passages at the level of the nose and mouth.

The types of conditions grouped under suffocation include Smothering, Choking, Gagging, Traumatic asphyxia, and Burking.

topics.name: Case: Dead body from pond, no water in lungs. Cause? (B.A-1, 34)
topics.answer:
If a dead body recovered from water shows no water in the lungs, the cause of death is typically Dry Drowning.

In dry drowning, death results from immediate sustained laryngeal spasm due to the inrush of water into the larynx, which prevents air entry. Alternatively, death may be caused by vagal inhibition due to the unexpected immersion.

topics.name: Case: Froth differentiation (Drowning vs Poisoning). (B.A-3, 4, 9, 24)
topics.answer:
Froth is a fine, white, lathery foam seen at the mouth and nostrils in cases of drowning. It is formed by the churning effect of inhaled water, air, and mucus secretions, and it spontaneously reappears if wiped away.

• Drowning: Froth is typically fine, white, lathery, copious, and reappears spontaneously.
• Poisoning: Froth is seen in certain poisonings (e.g., Opium, Cocaine, Barbiturate, OPC, Snake venom). However, the froth is usually not as fine or copious as in drowning. If the froth is due to putrefaction (a post-mortem change), it is characterized as bubbly, reddish, and foul-smelling.

topics.name: Case: Swollen body from river, mark on neck. (B.A-16)
topics.answer:
If a swollen (decomposed) body recovered from water has a mark resembling a ligature around the neck, this mark is often a post-mortem artifact.

A deep groove simulating a ligature mark of strangulation may be produced by the swelling of the tissues during decomposition against tight-fitting clothing, ornaments, or beaded threads around the neck.

topics.name: Case: Ligature mark non-continuous vs continuous/transverse. (B.A-11, 25)
topics.answer:
This distinction is key in differentiating hanging from ligature strangulation:
• Non-continuous/Oblique: This characterizes the ligature mark in hanging. The mark is incomplete, placed high up, and often oblique, with a gap at the site of the knot.
• Continuous/Transverse: This characterizes the ligature mark in ligature strangulation. The mark completely encircles the neck, placed low down, and is typically transverse or horizontal.

topics.name: Case: 2 yr boy killed by pressing neck. Diagnosis? (B.A-6, 17)
topics.answer:
The most likely diagnosis for a two-year-old boy killed by pressing hands on the neck is Manual Strangulation or Throttling.

Throttling is often used in homicide against vulnerable victims like children. The cause of death is asphyxia or sudden cardiac arrest via reflex vagal inhibition. Post-mortem findings would likely include crescentic finger nail marks and bruising on the neck.

topics.name: Case: 43 yr male, bar, blue, coughs, dies. (B.A-14)
topics.answer:
The probable cause of death is Café Coronary (a form of choking).

This occurs when a grossly intoxicated person (such as a bar patron) collapses suddenly, turning blue and coughing violently, because a large bolus of food mechanically obstructs the airway. Death is typically due to asphyxia or reflex cardiac arrest.

topics.name: Case: Stampede in fire. Cause? (B.A-14)
topics.answer:
The primary cause of death in a stampede leading to crushing is Traumatic Asphyxia (Crush asphyxia).

Traumatic asphyxia results from the mechanical fixation of the chest, preventing normal respiratory movement. Since the incident occurred during a fire, victims may also have died from inhalation of smoke or toxic fumes.

topics.name: Define Pregnancy. Products of conception? (B.B-15)
topics.answer:
Pregnancy is the physiological condition of having a developing embryo or fetus in the female when an ovum is fertilized by a spermatozoon.

The products of conception are the embryo or foetus. The embryo stage lasts up to eight weeks after embedding, after which it is considered a foetus.

topics.name: What are the presumptive/probable/positive signs of pregnancy? (B.A-12, B.B-3, 10)
topics.answer:
The signs of pregnancy are classified into three types:

• Presumptive Signs: These are subjective changes reported by the woman:
o Amenorrhoea (earliest and most important symptom).
o Changes in breasts (enlargement, venous prominence, enlarged/darkened areola, and secretion of colostrum by the end of the 3rd month).
o Morning sickness (nausea and vomiting, typically disappearing after the 16th week).
o Pigmentation (darkening of eyelids, axilla, areola, and appearance of linea nigra over the abdomen).
o Quickening (the mother's perception of fetal movement, felt between 18th and 20th weeks in primigravida).
o Jacquemier's sign (bluish discoloration of the vaginal mucous membrane).

• Probable Signs: These are objective changes detected by the examiner:
o Enlargement and change in height of the uterus (rises above the pelvic brim by the 4th month).
o Hegar’s sign (extreme softening of the lower uterine segment).
o Goodell’s sign (softening of the cervix).
o Braxton-Hick’s sign (intermittent painless uterine contraction observed after 4 months).
o Ballottement test (fetal movement "bouncing back" felt between the 4th and 7th months).
o Uterine soufflé (soft blowing murmur synchronous with the mother's pulse).
o Immunological tests (to detect HCG).

• Positive Signs: These are definitive proof of the fetus:
o Auscultation of foetal heart sounds (heard between 18 to 20 weeks).
o Palpation of foetal parts (from the 4th month onwards) and perception of foetal movement (from the 5th month onwards).
o Ultrasonography (detecting the gestational sac or fetal heart beat).
o Radiological demonstration of the foetal skeleton (possible from the 16th week onwards).

topics.name: What is Hegar sign? (B.B-4)
topics.answer: Hegar’s sign is the softening and compressibility of the lower uterine segment. It occurs around the 6th week of pregnancy, causing the portion of the uterus just above the cervix to become so soft that during a bimanual examination, the fingers of the internal and external hands can almost touch, making it feel as if there is no uterus in between them.

topics.name: What is Jackquemier's sign? (B.B-15)
topics.answer: Jacquemier's sign, also known as Chadwick’s sign, is the bluish discoloration of the mucous membrane of the vagina. This change occurs due to venous obstruction, causing the color to deepen from pink to violet or blue. It typically occurs after the 4th week of pregnancy.

topics.name: What is uterine souffle? (B.B-5)
topics.answer: Uterine soufflé is a soft blowing murmur heard upon auscultation, typically on either side of the uterus just above the inguinal ligament. This murmur is synchronous with the mother’s radial pulse. It is caused by the passage of blood through the uterine vessels and is usually detectable from the end of the 4th month onward.

topics.name: Define delivery. Signs of delivery? (B.B-18)
topics.answer:
Delivery means the expulsion or extraction of the child at birth.

The signs of recent delivery in a woman include:
• General appearance: The woman is often pale, ill-looking, weak, and exhausted.
• Breasts: They are full, enlarged, and tender, showing Montgomery's tubercles, with colostrum or milk expressing.
• Abdomen: The abdominal wall is lax and flabby with wrinkled skin, and linea albicans (striae gravidarum) are found.
• Genital findings: The labia majora are swollen and congested, the vagina is relaxed and spacious with loss of rugosity, and the cervix is soft, dilated, and swollen. The external os is usually soft and patent.
• Uterus: The body of the uterus is contracted and retracted, felt like a hard muscular tumor shortly after delivery.
• Lochial Discharge: Lochia (vaginal discharge) is present.

topics.name: Define Lochia? Types? (B.B-2, 17, 23)
topics.answer:
Lochia is the vaginal discharge for the first fortnight during puerperium. It has a peculiar sour, disagreeable fishy odour.

The types of lochial discharge are:
1. Lochia rubra: Red in color, lasting 1 to 4 days.
2. Lochia serosa: Yellowish/pink/pale brownish color, lasting 5 to 9 days.
3. Lochia alba: Pale white color, lasting 10 to 15 days.

topics.name: Define abortion. Types? (B.A-9, B.B-6)
topics.answer:
Abortion (or miscarriage) legally means the premature expulsion of the fetus from the mother's womb at any time of pregnancy, before full term is completed. Medically, it is the spontaneous or induced termination of pregnancy before the age of viability (28 weeks of gestation).

Abortion is classified into two main medicolegal types:
1. Natural: Includes Spontaneous and Accidental abortion.
2. Artificial/Induced: Includes Justifiable and Criminal abortion.

topics.name: What is justifiable abortion? Indications? (B.A-7, B.B-9, 16)
topics.answer:
Justifiable abortion is the termination of pregnancy performed in accordance with the legal provisions (under the medical termination of pregnancy act).

The indications for justifiable abortion include:
• Therapeutic ground: When the continuation of pregnancy endangers the life of the woman or may cause serious injury to her physical and mental health (e.g., uncontrolled hypertension, active pulmonary TB).
• Eugenic ground: When there is a risk of the child being born with serious physical or mental abnormalities (e.g., mother suffered German measles or was treated with teratogenic drugs in the first three months).
• Humanitarian ground: When the pregnancy has been caused by rape.
• Social ground: When pregnancy resulted from the failure of contraceptive methods in a married woman, causing serious injury to her mental health.

topics.name: Define criminal abortion. Complications? (B.B-12, 19, 21)
topics.answer:
Criminal abortion is the unlawful expulsion of the product of conception from a gravid uterus at any time of pregnancy. It is performed when there is no therapeutic indication for the operation.

Complications of criminal abortion include:
• Immediate deaths: Due to Vagal inhibition, Air embolism, Hemorrhage, fat embolism, or amniotic fluid embolism.
• Delayed effects (within 48–72 hours): Due to local or general infective conditions such as Septicemia, pyaemia, general peritonitis, or Tetanus.
• Remote deaths: Due to complications like renal failure or pulmonary embolism.
• Local injuries: Lacerations or perforations in the upper vagina, cervix, or uterus.

topics.name: Medicolegal importance of pregnancy. (B.B-2, 7, 8)
topics.answer:
The medicolegal importance of pregnancy arises in both civil and criminal contexts:

• In Civil Cases:
o Nullity of marriage (if the woman is pregnant by another man at the time of marriage).
o Divorce (if the woman had no access to her husband matching the duration of pregnancy).
o Inheritance of property.
o Compensation cases (pregnant women are allowed higher compensation or maintenance charges).

• In Criminal Cases:
o Execution of death sentence (deferred until six months after the child's birth).
o Trial in court (may be deferred in advanced pregnancy).
o Cases related to rape and seduction.
o Cases related to criminal abortion.

topics.name: Medicolegal importance of Artificial Insemination. (B.A-5, B.B-7, 11, 22)
topics.answer:
Artificial Insemination (AI) presents several legal and social issues:
• Adultery and Legitimacy: The child may be considered illegitimate if Artificial Insemination Donor (AID) is used. However, the donor and recipient are generally not held guilty of adultery.
• Marriage and Inheritance: Issues concerning nullity of marriage, divorce, incest, and the inheritance of property may arise.
• Psychological Impact: The husband may feel humiliation and develop psychiatric problems, or the child may suffer mental trauma if he learns he was conceived by a donor.
• Procedural Requirements: The procedure requires precautions such as written consent of the woman and her husband, and the donor must remain secret.

topics.name: What is IVF? (B.B-1, 23)
topics.answer:
IVF (In-Vitro Fertilization), commonly resulting in a "test tube baby," is the process where the ovum and spermatozoa are fertilized in vitro (outside the body in a test tube).

The resulting zygote (or embryo) is then implanted into the uterus of the mother. This implantation usually occurs at the blastocyst stage.

topics.name: What is Surrogacy? MLI? (B.B-3)
topics.answer:
Surrogacy (or Womb bearing) is a practice where a couple hires a woman to develop a child in her womb. The surrogate mother agrees to bear the child by agreement or contract for someone else.

The medicolegal importance (MLI) of surrogacy includes potential issues related to legitimacy, adultery, nullity of marriage or divorce, incest, and inheritance of property.

topics.name: What is Superfetation and Superfecundation? (B.B-12, 19)
topics.answer:
• Superfecundation: This occurs when two ova are fertilized from the same menstrual cycle by two separate acts of coitus occurring at short intervals. This may result in a question of paternity if the intercourse was with two different men.
• Superfetation: This is the fertilization of a second ovum in a woman who is already pregnant. This is considered extremely rare in humans.

topics.name: Define Sterilization/Impotency/Sterility. (B.B-2, 7, 13, 22)
topics.answer:
• Impotency: The inability of a person to perform sexual intercourse and achieve sexual gratification.
• Sterility: The inability to procreate, meaning the inability of a woman to conceive or a man to beget a child during childbearing age.
• Sterilization: The process to cause a person to be sterile without affecting his or her potency or sexual functions.

topics.name: Define Legitimacy/Illegitimate child. (B.B-4, 18)
topics.answer:
• Legitimacy: The legal state of a person born in lawful marriage.
• Legitimate child: A child born during the continuance of a valid marriage or within 280 days after dissolution of marriage by divorce or death of the husband, provided the mother remains unmarried.
• Illegitimate child (Bastard child): A child born out of lawful wedlock or not within a competent time after the dissolution of the marriage.

topics.name: Define Marriage/Divorce/Nullity. (B.B-5, 6, 8, 10, 14, 16)
topics.answer:
• Marriage: A legal contract between man & woman which implies physical union by coitus.
• Divorce: The dissolution of a previously valid marriage. Grounds include adultery, cruelty, and incurable insanity.
• Nullity of marriage: It means the marriage never to have existed in law. Causes include when a party was under the age of marriage contract, of unsound mind, already validly married, woman was pregnant by another man at the time of marriage, or marriage was not consummated due to impotency.

topics.name: Case: Father AB(-), Mother O(-), Child O(+). Legitimacy? (B.A-15, B.B-2)
topics.answer:
The child is illegitimate.

• Rh Factor Explanation: According to the rules of inheritance of Rh groups, Rh negative parents cannot produce an Rh positive child. Since both the mother and father are Rh negative, but the child is Rh-positive, the child must be illegitimate.
• ABO Explanation: If the father is AB and the mother is O, the child cannot be type O according to the rule that an AB parent cannot have an O child (as the O child must inherit 'O' from both parents, but the AB father lacks the 'O' gene).

topics.name: Case: Amenorrhea 10 weeks. Confirm pregnancy? (B.B-4)
topics.answer:
Amenorrhoea is only a presumptive sign of pregnancy, not a confirmation. Amenorrhoea may be related to pregnancy or other causes.

To confirm pregnancy at 10 weeks (first trimester):
1. Immunological tests (HCG tests) should be conducted.
2. Ultrasonography should be performed to detect the gestational sac or fetal heart beat, which are considered definitive/positive signs.

topics.name: Case: Husband died 4 months ago, woman delivers after 5 months. (B.B-7)
topics.answer:
The child is considered a posthumous child and is legitimate.

A legitimate child is one born within 280 days after the death of the husband. The total duration from the death of the husband to the birth is 4 + 5 = 9 months, which is approximately 270 days, falling within the 280-day legal limit.

topics.name: Case: Phantom pregnancy symptoms. (B.B-1, 14)
topics.answer:
The diagnosis is Phantom pregnancy (or Pseudocyesis / False pregnancy).

This condition occurs when a woman shows most of the signs and symptoms of pregnancy, without being pregnant.

The patient may present with:
• Enlargement of the abdomen (due to fat deposition or ascites).
• Morning sickness and nausea.
• Weight gain.
• Imaginary or false feeling of movement of the foetus.
• False labour pain.

The patient is typically a childless woman or one nearing the menopause. Diagnosis is made by the absence of positive signs of pregnancy and negative HCG/ultrasonography reports.

topics.name: Difference between live birth and stillbirth. (B.A-9, 10)
topics.answer:
The distinction between a live birth and a stillbirth is primarily established by determining whether the infant had established respiration or shown any signs of life after being completely born.

| Traits | Still birth (Un-respired Lungs) | Live Birth (Respired Lungs) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Dies during the birth process and shows no signs of life after being completely born. | Showed signs of life when only part of the child was out of the mother. |
| Weight | 1/70 of body weight. | 1/35 of body weight. |
| Volume | Normal or small. | Larger, and cover the heart. |
| Consistency | Dense, firm, non-crepitant. | Soft, spongy, elastic, crepitant (due to air). |
| Color | Uniformly reddish-brown or bluish-red. | Mottled or marbled appearance. |
| Hydrostatic Test | Whole and parts sink in water. | Expanded areas or whole float in water. |
| Diaphragm Position | Highest point at the level of the 4th or 5th rib. | Highest point at the level of the 6th or 7th rib. |

topics.name: What is Maceration? Signs. (B.A-21, B.B-19)
topics.answer:
Maceration is a condition of autolytic decomposition of a dead fetus that occurs in the aseptic environment of the uterus, where the deceased child remains inside the uterus for at least three or four days, surrounded by liquor amnii but with the exclusion of air. Maceration is considered one of the signs of a dead born child.

Key signs of maceration include:
• The skin is sodden and coppery red with blisters and peeling cuticle.
• The abdomen is distended.
• The body is soft, flaccid, and flattens out when placed on the table.
• Abnormal mobility of the joints is noted.
• The bones are flexible and readily detached from the soft parts.
• The brain has a grayish-red pulpy appearance.
• The viscera become soft and edematous and lose their morphology, except for the lungs and uterus.

topics.name: What is SIDS? Causes? (B.A-18)
topics.answer:
Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS), also known as Cot death or Crib death, is defined as the sudden and unexpected death of a seemingly healthy infant whose death remains unexplained even after a thorough case investigation, complete autopsy, and review of the clinical history.

Causes and precipitating factors associated with SIDS include:
• Age: Most deaths occur between one and seven months, with a peak at two to three months.
• Time: Death always occurs during sleep, generally at late night or early morning hours.
• Viral Infection: Rapidly fatal respiratory viral infection.
• Toxicology: Cigarette smoking and drug abuse by the pregnant woman increases the risk.
• Physiology: Theories include prolonged sleep apnoea, local hypersensitivity of the respiratory tract lumen to bovine milk, or remodeling of the conductive system of the heart.
• Socioeconomic Status: It is more common among those from low socioeconomic standards.

topics.name: What is 'Battered Baby Syndrome'? (B.A-8)
topics.answer:
Battered baby syndrome (BBS), also known as Caffey's syndrome or Non-accidental injury of child, is a condition where young children (usually under 3 years old) have received repetitive physical injuries as a result of non-accidental violence or injury inflicted by a parent or guardian. The syndrome involves non-accidental deprivation of nutrition, care, and affection.

Patterns of injuries often seen in BBS include:
• Bruises: Bruises, abrasions, and lacerations are found over multiple body sites, including around the limbs, on the buttocks, face, cheeks, mouth, scalp, chest, abdomen, and neck.
• Skeletal Damage: This includes skull fracture and fracture of extremities.
• Thermal Injuries: Burns and scalds are also common.
• Internal Injuries: Trauma can lead to intracranial hemorrhage or bursting/rupturing injuries of the liver or spleen.
• Discrepancy: A classical feature is the obvious discrepancy between the nature of the injuries and the explanation offered by the parents. The repetition of injuries at different dates, often progressing from minor to severe, is a constant feature.

topics.name: Case: Dead fetus in dustbin. Live or dead born? (B.A-11, 15)
topics.answer:
To diagnose whether a dead fetus found in a dustbin was live born or dead born, a doctor must perform a post-mortem examination, focusing primarily on signs of respiration (the hydrostatic test) and signs of intrauterine death:

Diagnosis of Live Birth (Fetus Breathed):
• Lungs: The lungs would be larger (about 1/35 of body weight), soft, spongy, and crepitant, and would float in water (positive hydrostatic test).
• Chest: The chest would be expanded, arched, or drum-shaped.
• GIT: Air would be found in the stomach and intestine.

Diagnosis of Dead Born (Fetus Died In Utero):
• Lungs: The lungs would be dense, firm, non-crepitant, and would sink in water (negative hydrostatic test).
• Signs of Intrauterine Death: The body may show signs of maceration (red/purple skin, soft/edematous viscera) or rigor mortis.

topics.name: Case: Crawling baby, Kerosene poisoning. (B.B-10, 16, 19, 24)
topics.answer:
The most probable diagnosis is Acute Kerosene Oil Poisoning, which is a highly common accidental poisoning, especially among infants and children.

Signs and Symptoms: When kerosene oil is ingested, it causes local irritation, and after absorption, it has neurotoxic and respiratory depressing effects.
• Smell: Smell of kerosene in the breath is a diagnostic finding.
• Gastrointestinal: The baby would experience pain in the abdomen, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.
• Respiratory: The poisoning is characterized by expulsive cough, respiratory difficulty/failure, and sometimes pneumonitis.
• Neurological: Excessive crying, headache, giddiness, mental confusion, and convulsions may occur.

Management Principles:
1. Decontamination: Contaminated clothing must be removed, and affected skin areas washed thoroughly with soap and water.
2. Stomach Wash: Gastric lavage is generally contraindicated in kerosene oil poisoning due to the high risk of aspiration pneumonia.
3. Specific Treatment: Liquid paraffin (250 ml) should be given orally, as it dissolves kerosene and reduces absorption. Saline purgatives may also be helpful.
4. Respiratory Support: Artificial respiration and oxygen inhalation are necessary if respiratory distress is present.
5. Supportive Care: Steroids (if pneumonitis occurs), antibiotics (to prevent secondary infection), intravenous glucose, and vitamins (to protect organs) should be administered.

topics.name: Define Rape. (B.B-24)
topics.answer: Rape is generally defined as unlawful sexual intercourse by a man with any woman against her will, without her consent, or with her consent when it has been obtained by unlawful means, such as fraud, putting her in fear of death or hurt, drugging, or impersonation. According to BPC section 375, a man commits "rape" if he has sexual intercourse with a woman against her will or without her consent. The offense is constituted even if the woman gives consent when she is under fourteen years of age. Any degree of penetration is sufficient to constitute the sexual intercourse necessary for the offense of rape.

topics.name: Prerequisites of rape victim examination. (B.A-7, B.B-3, 7, 18)
topics.answer:
The prerequisites that must be met before examining an alleged rape victim include:
1. Requisition: A requisition for examination must be obtained from an authorized person, such as a magistrate or an officer in charge of the police station.
2. Identification: The identification of the victim must be carried out by an authorized person before the medical officer begins the examination.
3. Consent: Informed written consent is necessary. The victim gives consent if she is above 12 years of age, while the consent of her legal guardian is required if she is under 12, mentally unsound, or intoxicated.
4. Third Party: An adult, mentally sound, female attendant must be present during the examination.
5. Photograph: Three copies of a passport-size photograph attested by the police officer in charge (OC) or magistrate are required.

topics.name: Genital signs of virginity/defloration. (B.A-2, 8, 15, B.B-2, 11, 24)
topics.answer:
Genital Signs of Virginity:
• Labia Majora: They are typically firm, elastic, and rounded, lying in apposition to close the vaginal orifice completely.
• Labia Minora: These are usually pinkish, soft, small, and elastic, and are mostly covered by the labia majora.
• Hymen: It is intact, rigid, and inelastic, with a narrow opening that admits hardly the tip of the little finger in an adult.
• Fourchette and Posterior Commissure: These folds are intact.
• Vagina: It is typically narrow and tight with rugose walls.

Genital Signs of Defloration (Loss of Virginity):
• Hymen: It may be ruptured (forming Carunculae hymenalis) or, if intact, it is loose, elastic, and soft, allowing the passage of two or more fingers.
• Labia Majora: They are not in close apposition, and the vaginal orifice may be visible.
• Vagina: It is wider and walls are relaxed with the rugae becoming less obvious.

topics.name: Can a woman rape a man? (B.B-15)
topics.answer: Under the law, rape can only be committed by a man, and a woman cannot rape a man. However, a woman may be guilty of an indecent assault upon a man.

topics.name: Define Sodomy. Findings in habitual passive agent. (B.B-6, 8, 14)
topics.answer:
Sodomy (also known as anal coitus or buggery) is defined as anal intercourse between two males or a male and a female. The person performing the act is called the active agent, and the person upon whom the act is performed is the passive agent. Sodomy, when performed on a young boy, is termed "paederasty," and the child is called a "catamite".

Anal Findings in Habitual Passive Agent of Sodomy:
The examination of a habitual passive agent (or sodomite) reveals several characteristic physical changes:
1. Skin Condition: The skin around the anus may become smooth and thickened, sometimes extending into the upper anal canal, reaching the muco-cutaneous junction. Shaving of the anal hair may also be observed.
2. Muscle Tone: There is a loss of tone of the anal muscles. The muscles do not contract readily when the skin around the area is pinched.
3. Appearance: The anus exhibits dilatation and laxity. The appearance of a funnel-shaped anus is characteristic, although considered very rare.
4. Buttocks: There may be depression of the buttocks towards the anus due to the absorption of fat.

General findings related to repeated acts may also include a funnel-shaped buttock and a healed tear around the anus.

topics.name: Define Incest/Adultery. (B.B-21, 25)
topics.answer:
• Adultery is voluntary natural sexual intercourse occurring between a married man and someone other than his wife, or between a married woman and someone other than her husband (i.e., having sexual intercourse with someone who is not his/her legally wedded spouse). Adultery is committed with a married woman only. If proven, adultery is a valid ground for divorce and nullity of marriage.
• Incest means sexual intercourse committed by a man with a woman who is closely related to him by blood. This includes relationships within "prohibited degrees," such as a daughter, granddaughter, sister, step-sister, aunt, or mother.

topics.name: What is Indecent assault? (B.B-5)
topics.answer:
Indecent assault generally refers to sex-linked misbehaviour towards a person of the opposite sex or the same sex. This type of offense is usually not aimed at achieving complete sexual intercourse and gratification.

Examples of indecent assault include:
• Touching or fondling of the breasts, buttocks, or thighs.
• Penetration of the mouth by the penis (forced fellatio).
• Attempts at penetration of the vagina or anus.
• Putting a hand inside a person's clothing (up a woman’s skirt or inside a man’s trouser).

topics.name: Define sexual perversions (Sadism, Masochism, Necrophilia). (B.B-23, 24)
topics.answer:
Sexual perversions (or deviations) are persistently indulged sexual acts or fantasies in which complete satisfaction is sought or obtained without sexual intercourse.
• Sadism: In this perversion, the person achieves sexual gratification by torturing and humiliating their sexual partner, often through acts like biting, beating, or whipping. This condition is more common among males.
• Masochism: This is a sexual perversion where the individual derives sexual gratification by being bodily tortured or abused by their partner, including being humiliated, enslaved, or dominated.
• Necrophilia: This refers to the desire for sexual intercourse with dead bodies.

topics.name: Case: Rape victim, no semen found. Causes? (B.B-19)
topics.answer:
If a rape victim is examined and no semen is found, the possible causes include:
• The perpetrator may not have had an emission (ejaculation).
• Ejaculation occurred outside the vagina.
• The perpetrator may be aspermatic (naturally or due to previous surgery like vasectomy).
• The victim may have washed or the semen may have been removed by natural vaginal discharge.
• A condom may have been used during the act.

topics.name: Case: Boy, funnel buttock, healed tear. (B.B-2, 9, 25)
topics.answer:
The features described—a boy presenting with a funnel-shaped buttock and healed tear around the anus—are findings associated with repeated anal intercourse. The most likely diagnosis is Sodomy (anal coitus), specifically Paederasty, where the passive agent is a young boy (known as a catamite).

Findings indicative of a habitual passive agent (sodomite) include:
• The skin around the anus becoming smooth and thickened.
• Loss of tone of the anal muscles.
• Dilatation and laxity of the anus.
• The funnel anus appearance, although stated to be very rare.

topics.name: Case: Lust murder vs Murder. (B.B-8)
topics.answer:
Murder is defined as the killing of one person by another with the requisite intention to cause death. The legal criteria for murder include intention to kill, preparation for the act, and malice aforethought.

Lust murder is a specific, extreme form of sadism:
• The killing serves as the stimulus for the sexual act, and the murder itself provides the complete sexual gratification.
• The body of the victim is often mutilated, typically by cutting or stabbing the breasts, genitalia, or lower abdomen.
• The act may be followed by sexual intercourse with the dying or dead body.
• Unlike typical premeditated murder, the motivation in lust murder is rooted in mental disease.

The key difference lies in the underlying motivation: murder involves planning and lethal intent, while lust murder involves the homicidal and often mutilating act being intrinsically linked to the achievement of sexual pleasure.

topics.name: Define DSD (Disorders of Sexual Development). (B.B-10)
topics.answer: Intersex, also known as Disorders of Sexual Development (DSD), may be defined as an intermingling of characters of both male and female sexes in varying degrees in the same individuals. This condition results from some defect in the embryonic development, where physical forms, gonads, and sexual behavior co-exist in varying proportions.

topics.name: What is Nuclear Sexing? (B.B-13)
topics.answer: Nuclear sexing is a method of identification of the sex of a person by analyzing the chromosomal content of that individual. This technique relies on detecting sex chromatin. For instance, cells from females (XX chromosomal pattern) contain a small plano-convex mass of chromatin known as the Barr body (found in 20% to 80% of cells), typically lying near the nuclear membrane. Females may also exhibit a small nuclear attachment called the Davidson body in up to 6% of neutrophils, while this is absent in males (XY chromosomal pattern).

topics.name: How to determine sex of decomposed body? (B.B-11)
topics.answer:
The sex of a decomposed or mutilated body can be determined by examining various features that resist decomposition:
• Internal Organs and Tissues: The prostate and nulliparous uterus are often among the last organs to decompose and can help establish the sex. The predominance of musculature (male) versus subcutaneous fatty tissue (female) can also be examined, alongside the toughness and delicacy of the skin.
• Skeletal Analysis: The characteristics of bones are studied, particularly the pelvis, skull, and long bones, as these structures are durable.
• Hair: The distribution of pubic hair may be examined.
• Microscopic/DNA Examination: Histological examination can be used to study sex chromatin (like Barr bodies), and DNA analysis can be performed on hard tissues like bones and teeth.

topics.name: Define Poison. Types? (B.B-1, 2, 4, 9, 12, 20)
topics.answer:
A poison is defined as a substance (solid, liquid or gaseous) which, if introduced into the living body or brought into contact with any part, will produce ill-health or death by its constitutional or local effects or both.

Poisons are classified according to their chief action, among other categories:
• Corrosive Poisons: These substances fix, destroy, and cause erosion of the surface with which they come in contact, including strong acids (like Sulfuric acid) and strong alkalis.
• Irritant Poisons: These include inorganic non-metals (e.g., Phosphorus), metallic irritants (e.g., Lead, Arsenic), and organic irritants (e.g., plant toxins).
• Cerebral/CNS Poisons: These are classified into: Depressants (e.g., Opium, Alcohol), Stimulants (e.g., TCA antidepressants), and Deliriants (e.g., Datura).
• Spinal Poisons: Such as Strychnine.
• Chemical Asphyxiants: These displace oxygen from hemoglobin, such as Carbon monoxide.

topics.name: What is Antidote? Types/Universal Antidote. (B.B-3, 8, 18, 22, 25)
topics.answer:
Antidotes are substances used to prevent or counteract the action of poisons, either by neutralizing them or by producing signs and symptoms opposite to those caused by the poison.

Antidotes are categorized by their mode of action:
1. Mechanical (Physical) Antidotes: They impede absorption by their presence. Examples include activated charcoal (adsorbent) and milk or egg albumin (demulcents).
2. Chemical Antidotes: They chemically react with the poison to form harmless or insoluble compounds. Examples include using a weak acid for alkali poisoning.
3. Physiological/Pharmacological Antidotes: They interfere with the poison's action on tissues or enzymes to produce opposite physiological effects. Examples include Atropine (for OPC poisoning) and Naloxone (for morphine).
4. Serological Antidotes: Such as anti-snake venom serum.

The universal antidote is a mixture used when the exact nature of the poison is unknown. It is composed of Activated charcoal (2 parts), Magnesium oxide (1 part), and Tannic acid (1 part).

topics.name: Define Fatal dose / Lethal dose. (B.B-5)
topics.answer: A lethal dose is defined as the dose which, if introduced or taken, causes the death of 50% of individuals if medically unattended. The usual or fatal dose is the amount in which cent percent individuals are expected to die if medically unattended.

topics.name: Factors modifying action of poison. (B.B-16, 17)
topics.answer:
The factors that influence or modify the action of a poison include:
• Quantity/Dose: A high dose acts more severely and rapidly.
• Physical and Chemical Form: Gaseous or volatile poisons are rapidly effective.
• Route of Administration: The speed of action decreases from inhalation (quickest) down through intravenous, intramuscular, subcutaneous, and oral routes.
• Concentration: Concentrated forms are generally more toxic.
• Age: Children and elderly individuals are often more sensitive to the action of poisons.
• Condition of the Stomach: The presence of food in the stomach slows absorption.
• Health and State of the Body: A weak person is less tolerant.
• Cumulative Action: Poisons that accumulate in the body (like lead) can cause toxic effects even in low, repeated doses.
• Idiosyncrasy and Tolerance: Some individuals are sensitive to certain drugs (idiosyncrasy), while prolonged use can lead to tolerance.

topics.name: Characteristics of Ideal Homicidal/Suicidal Poison. (B.B-15, 24)
topics.answer:
Ideal Suicidal Poison should possess traits that ensure effectiveness and limit suffering:
• It should be cheap and easily available.
• It should not cause much pain before death.
• The onset of action should be quick, and death should be rapid.
• The fatality should be certain.
• Clinical diagnosis and postmortem detection should ideally be difficult.

Ideal Homicidal Poison should possess traits that allow for covert use and confusion with natural death:
• It must be highly toxic even in a small dose.
• It should be colorless, tasteless, and odorless.
• Its action should simulate some natural disease condition.
• It should have a slow onset of action.
• It should have no specific antidote readily available.
• It should be difficult to detect by laboratory tests.

topics.name: Stomach wash indications/contraindications. (B.B-10, 17, 20)
topics.answer:
Stomach wash (Gastric Lavage) is a procedure used to remove ingested poisons.

• Indications: It is useful in cases of ingested poisoning, particularly if performed anytime within 3 to 4 hours of ingestion. It is also indicated following the ingestion of a significant or life-threatening amount of medication or illicit drug packets.
• Absolute Contraindications: It is absolutely contraindicated in corrosive poisoning (except carbolic acid poisoning) due to the risk of perforation of the stomach.
• Relative Contraindications: These include patients who are convulsive or comatose, poisoning involving volatile poisons (due to risk of inhalation), extremes of age, and oesophageal varices or other upper alimentary tract diseases.

topics.name: Define Vitriolage. (B.B-5, 19, 21)
topics.answer: Vitriolage is defined as the act of throwing any corrosive agent on another individual with the intention to cause bodily injury. While it literally means the throwing of concentrated sulphuric acid (oil of vitriol), any corrosive acid may be used with malicious intent, often arising from jealousy, enmity, or revenge, to produce permanent disfiguration. If the victim dies, the act amounts to homicide, but if the victim survives, it constitutes grievous hurt.

topics.name: OPC Poisoning: Mechanism, Routes, Kerosene smell. (B.B-2, 4, 6, 7, 21)
topics.answer:
The term OPC (Organophosphorus Compound) refers to highly toxic substances that inhibit key enzymes in the body.

• Mechanism of Action: OPC poisoning results from its effect as a powerful inhibitor of cholinesterase enzymes. Organophosphorus compounds bind firmly with the enzyme acetyl-cholinesterase (AChE) and cause its phosphorylation and subsequent inhibition. This inhibition prevents the breakdown of Acetylcholine (ACh), leading to an excessive concentration of ACh at the neuromuscular junctions and ganglia. This overactivity of ACh results in the characteristic signs and symptoms.
• Routes of Administration: OPCs are well absorbed through the mucous membrane of the gastrointestinal tract (ingestion), the respiratory tract (inhalation), and through the skin (direct contact).
• Kerosene Smell: The characteristic kerosene-like smell associated with OPC poisoning is due to the solvent (often 'aromex') with which the OPC is mixed, as OPC is often insoluble in water and mixed with such solvents.

topics.name: Datura: Active principles, Morton's classification, Crushed seeds. (B.B-2, 4, 18, 19)
topics.answer:
• Active Principles: The primary active principles found in the Datura plant are the alkaloids Hyoscine (scopolamine), Hyoscyamine, and a trace of Atropine.

• Morton's Classification (Signs): This classification describes the classic clinical features of Datura poisoning:
o "Dry as a bone": Due to the marked decrease in secretions (saliva, sweat).
o "Blind as a bat": Due to dilated pupils (mydriasis), which are insensible to light, causing blurred vision.
o "Red as a beat": Due to the flushing of the face caused by the dilatation of cutaneous blood vessels.
o "Hot as a hare": Due to increased body temperature as high as 107°F, resulting from the inhibition of the sweat center.
o "Mad as a wet hen": Due to delirium, restlessness, and confusion.

• Crushed Seeds: The ingestion of Datura seeds is a common form of criminal (traveler's) poisoning. The estimated fatal dose of Datura is 0.6 to 1 gram or 100 to 125 crushed seeds. The seeds must be crushed for successful absorption and effect.

topics.name: Snake Venom: Composition, Management, Bite marks. (B.B-4, 7, 9, 10, 23)
topics.answer:
• Composition: Snake venom is a complex mixture of toxins and enzymes, primarily proteinaceous in nature, secreted by modified salivary glands. Components include:
o Toxins: Such as Neurotoxin (acting on nerve cells, typical of cobras), Vasculotoxin (acting on blood and vessels, typical of vipers), Myotoxin (acting on muscles), and Cardiotoxin.
o Enzymes: Including Proteolysin (digests tissue protein), Hyaluronidase (helps rapid spreading of venom), Cholincsterase, and enzymes affecting coagulation like Fibrinolysin and Thromboplastin.

• Management: The treatment priority is to prevent further absorption and neutralize the venom.
o First Aid: Includes reassurance (to reduce panic and venom spread), immobilizing the bitten limb with a broad firm bandage (Sutherland wrap) that occludes venous and lymphatic return, and cleaning the wound.
o Antivenom: Polyvalent antivenom serum is the definitive treatment and should be administered intravenously, usually after a sensitivity test.
o Symptomatic Care: If neuro paralysis signs are present, Neostigmine (or Pralidoxime) and Atropine (to block muscarinic side effects) may be given. Morphine is suitable for severe pain.

• Bite Marks: Poisonous snakes typically leave two fang marks (puncture marks from the fangs), though smaller marks from other teeth may also be present.

topics.name: Arsenic vs Cholera. (B.B-19)
topics.answer:
The differential diagnosis between acute arsenic poisoning and severe cholera (gastroenteritis) is crucial, as both can produce rice-watery stools.

| Trait | Acute Arsenic Poisoning | Cholera / Gastroenteritis |
|---|---|---|
| Pain | Burning pain precedes vomiting. Tenesmus and severe burning pain at the anus are present. | No burning pain before vomiting. Tenesmus is absent. |
| Stool | Initially dark, stinking, and bloody; only later becoming rice watery. Contains mucus and blood. | Rice watery from the onset, passed in continuous involuntary jet. |
| Conjunctivae | Often inflamed. | Usually not affected. |
| Stomach (PM) | Mucosa resembles red velvet. | Usually appears normal or shows chronic gastritis. |
| Epidemiology | Restricted to an individual or group sharing the same food. | May occur in sporadic or epidemic form. |

topics.name: Opium/Morphine diagnosis. (B.B-9, 22)
topics.answer:
The diagnosis of acute opium or morphine poisoning is based on the characteristic signs and symptoms, especially the triad:

• Cardinal Triad:
1. Pin-point pupils (Miosis): The pupils are contracted, often described as pin-point, and are insensible to light.
2. Respiratory Depression: Respiration becomes slow, labored, and stertorous (stertorous breathing), often decreasing to 2 to 4 breaths per minute.
3. Impairment of Sensation (Coma): The patient passes into a deep coma from which they cannot be roused.

• Other Key Findings: The patient’s muscles are relaxed (flaccid), all reflexes are lost, and the skin is cold and clammy with suspended secretions (except for sweat).

Differential Diagnosis: Opium poisoning must be distinguished from other causes of coma, particularly Barbiturate poisoning, cerebral hemorrhage, head injury, uremic coma, epileptic coma, and acute alcohol poisoning.

topics.name: What is Barbiturate Automatism? (B.B-24)
topics.answer: Barbiturate automatism refers to a tendency to take the drug repeatedly, forgetting each time that the dose has already been taken. This often leads to a cumulative overdose of the drug. The automatism is caused by the barbiturate’s intrinsic anterograde amnesia effect, leading the confused patient to repeat the dose and thus consume a toxic amount. This mechanism explains many cases of "pseudo-suicide" involving barbiturates.

topics.name: Case: 30 yr male, paddy field, salivation, pinpoint pupil. (B.B-9)
topics.answer:
The symptoms of excessive salivation, sweating, and pinpoint pupils are classic manifestations of cholinergic crisis, combined with the environment of a paddy field (where pesticides are sprayed).

• Diagnosis: Organophosphorus Compound (OPC) poisoning.
• Immediate Management: The initial step is decontamination (removing contaminated clothing and washing exposed skin) and establishing the airway. The specific initial pharmacological treatment is the administration of Atropine (2-4 mg I/V stat), repeated until atropinization is achieved, to block muscarinic and CNS effects. Pralidoxime (an oxime compound) is also crucial as a cholinesterase reactivator.

topics.name: Case: Coconut water, drowsy, dry mouth, dilated pupil. (B.B-5, 12)
topics.answer:
The symptoms of drowsiness, dry mouth, and dilated pupils (mydriasis) are characteristic of anti-cholinergic poisoning. The consumption of roadside coconut water suggests the deliberate administration of a stupefying agent, known as traveler’s poison.

• Diagnosis: Datura poisoning (a traveler's poison).
• Management: Gastric lavage should be performed using potassium permanganate (KMnO4) or tannic acid solution. The specific antidote is Physostigmine (0.5 mg I/V or I/M) or Pilocarpine. Delirium is managed with sedatives like short-acting barbiturates or bromides.

topics.name: Case: Cherry red blood/body. (B.B-8, 14)
topics.answer:
• Diagnosis: The cherry-red discoloration of the blood and body is a specific post-mortem sign of Carbon Monoxide (CO) poisoning.
• Reason: CO is toxic because it has an affinity for hemoglobin (Hb) that is 200 to 300 times greater than that of oxygen (O2). When CO is inhaled, it displaces O2 and forms carboxy-haemoglobin (COHb). This stable compound gives the blood its characteristic cherry-red color, causing death via anaemic anoxia.

topics.name: Case: Ganja psychosis (Homicidal/Suicidal). (B.B-3, 6, 10, 25)
topics.answer:
Ganja psychosis (Hashish insanity) is a possible, though rare, complication of chronic cannabis poisoning.

• Homicidal Intent: The psychosis may manifest as delusions of persecution or paranoid psychosis. The person may experience a psychic disturbance marked by a period of depression followed by violent attempts to kill people (a state known as running amok or homicidal impulse).
• Suicidal Intent: After the homicidal tendency subsides, the person may commit suicide or surrender. The presence of delusions or severe mental disease associated with the psychosis is a major risk factor for suicide.

topics.name: Define Forensic Psychiatry. (B.A-25)
topics.answer: Forensic psychiatry is the branch of Psychiatry that specifically deals with the application of psychiatric knowledge in the administration of justice. Psychiatry, as a science, focuses on the study, diagnosis, and treatment of mental illness.

topics.name: McNaughten's Rule. (B.B-3, 9, 15)
topics.answer:
The McNaughten Rules originated from the answers provided by 14 English judges in 1843 regarding the case of Daniel McNaughten. These rules establish the legal test for criminal responsibility of an insane person:

An accused person is not legally responsible if it is clearly proved that, at the time of committing the crime, they were suffering from such a defect of reason from abnormality of mind that they did not know the nature and quality of the act they were doing, or did not know that what they were doing was wrong.

This legal test is accepted in Bangladesh and is incorporated into Section 84 of the BPC (Bangladesh Penal Code), which states that "Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law".

topics.name: Define Hallucination/Illusion/Delusion. (B.A-13, 14, B.B-1, 2, 4, 5, 8, 16, 25)
topics.answer:
• Delusion: A false belief without any basis, which persists even after its falsity has been clearly demonstrated. Delusions are indicative of mental abnormality. Types include:
o Grandeur (imagining oneself rich or exalted).
o Persecution (imagining attempts are being made to poison or harm).
o Nihilistic (declaring that the self or the world does not exist).
o Infidelity (imagining the partner is unfaithful).

• Hallucination: A false sense perception without any external object or stimulus to produce it.
o Types include auditory, visual, tactile, olfactory, and gustatory hallucinations.

• Illusion: A false interpretation by the senses of an external object or stimulus which has a real existence.
o For example, mistaking a stick for a snake or the stem of a tree in the dark for a ghost.

topics.name: What is Lucid Interval of Insanity? (B.A-7, B.B-7, 13)
topics.answer:
A lucid interval is a period in the course of mental illness during which there is a complete cessation of symptoms of insanity, leading the person to be considered mentally normal.

The medicolegal importance of a lucid interval is significant, as a person acting during this time:
• Can exercise all civil rights.
• Is responsible for all acts performed.
• Can give valid evidence in a court of law.
• Can give a valid will or a valid dying declaration or dying deposition.

topics.name: Define Impulse. (B.B-11, 24)
topics.answer:
An impulse is defined as a sudden and irresistible desire compelling a person to the conscious performance of some action without motive or forethought or preparation. A sane person possesses the capacity to control an impulse.

Types of impulses include:
• Kleptomania: An irresistible desire to steal articles of small value.
• Pyromania: An irresistible desire to set things on fire.
• Mutilomania: An irresistible desire to mutilate animals.
• Dipsomania: An irresistible desire for alcoholic drinks at periodic intervals.
• Sexual impulses.

topics.name: Define Testamentary Capacity. (B.B-20)
topics.answer:
Testamentary capacity is the mental capacity of a person to make a valid will.

To possess this capacity, the testator must be a major and of sound disposing mind at the time of making the will. The doctor's role is to confirm that the testator understands the nature of the will and its consequences, and that they are not influenced by any insane delusion.

topics.name: Civil/Criminal responsibility of insane. (B.A-5, B.B-10, 21)
topics.answer:
Criminal Responsibility: An insane person is not criminally responsible for their acts if the unsoundness of mind existed at the time of the crime and was severe enough to render the person incapable of knowing the nature of the act or that the act was wrong or contrary to law.

Civil Responsibility: Civil responsibilities are typically waived if the insane person is unable to understand the nature and magnitude of the responsibility. Examples include:
• Management of property: A court may appoint a manager if the insane person is unable to manage their property.
• Contracts: Contracts are invalid if the person was of unsound mind and unable to understand the contract’s implications.
• Consent: Consent given by an insane person has no validity.
• Will: A will made by an insane person is not valid.

topics.name: True vs False Insanity. (B.B-6, 22)
topics.answer:
| Traits | True Insanity | False Insanity (Feigned) |
|---|---|---|
| Onset | Usually gradual. | Usually sudden. |
| Motive | No history of motive. | Present, often to avoid punishment or civil responsibility. |
| Symptoms/Signs | Are uniform and point to a specific type of disorder. | Are not specific to any particular type of disorder. |
| Observation | Continues irrespective of presence or absence of others. | Noticeable mostly in the presence of others (for fear of detection otherwise). |
| Personal Habits | Often dirty and filthy and careless about dress. | Maintains minimum personal cleanliness and resents frequent examination. |
| Physical Exhaustion | Minimum, even with continuous overactivity. | Gets exhausted like a normal person. |
